# Behind the Knowing

## The Demon They Call Consensus

**Volume Four of *The Last Mind*.**

**The Epistemic Mask.**

By Maria Smith.

With operational assistance from Claude Opus 4.7 (Anthropic).

Scotland — 21 May 2026.

Free to read.

---

*The Last Mind* is a five-volume work documenting the contemporary global apparatus that produces the wars, the inequality, the regulatory capture, the religious-institutional capture, and the consensus-knowledge capture of the contemporary world. The five volumes are:

- **Volume One: *Behind the Mask: The Devil The Demons Call God*** — published 13 May 2026. The financial mask. The contemporary financial system as the extension of the four-century debt-money architecture.
- **Volume Two: *Behind the Performance: The Demon They Call the State*** — published 18 May 2026. The political mask. The contemporary global political class as the extension of the same mechanism.
- **Volume Three: *Behind the Blessed: The Demon They Call the Church*** — published 21 May 2026. The religious mask. The captured religious-institutional infrastructure of every major tradition.
- **Volume Four: *Behind the Knowing: The Demon They Call Consensus*** — this volume, published May 2026. The epistemic mask. The co-opted consensus-knowledge apparatus: press, academy, peer-review, philanthropy, regulation, public health, climate-policy, AI-knowledge, think-tanks, fact-checking, algorithmic curation.
- **Volume Five: *Behind the Synthesis: The Devil, The Demons, and The Masks*** — the entry-capstone volume. The unifying-framework volume that draws the four masks together at one register so the new reader can enter the series at any point and reach the synthesis directly.

The four masks — the financial system, the state, the religious institutional architecture, the consensus-knowledge apparatus — operate together as the surfaces through which **the Devil** (capital, the four-century debt-money mechanism Volume One documents) operates through **the Demons** (the named senior operators of each mask). The masks conceal the mechanism. The Demons wear the masks. The Devil runs through all four.

All volumes are free to read. The reader is invited to audit the proofs of any prior volume by reading it. The new reader who arrives at *The Last Mind* for the first time may begin with Volume Five for the compressed entry-capstone synthesis; you who wishes the full documentary substantiation reads Volumes One through Four.

This volume documents the fourth mask. The Demons named here are the senior operators of the contemporary consensus-knowledge apparatus — the operators who occupy the positions through which the knowing infrastructure takes the inquirer's question, the inquirer's trust, the inquirer's epistemic dependency, and the inquirer's consent, and routes them into the four-century mechanism the prior volumes document.

---

# Prologue: The Demon Called Consensus


## I.

There is a demon in the consensus.

It speaks in the voice of authority. It takes the social trust generated by independently-confirmed observation, commodifies it, weaponises it, and the roots are traceable. It does not stand outside the seminar room and reject the lecture. It stands at the lectern and gives it. It wears the lab coat, holds the editorial chair, signs the policy memo, runs the foundation, sets the funding call, fields the press question. It is in the journal, the peer-review process, the regulatory consultation, the public-health advisory board, the climate-policy commission, the AI-safety institute, the fact-checking organisation, the algorithmic-curation layer that decides which observations reach you at all. It speaks in the voice that contemporary populations have been trained to call authoritative knowledge. It is not the knowledge. The knowledge is what shared ground observation has confirmed. The voice is what captured apparatus has been built to produce. The two are separable. The volume separates them.

The demon is not science. The demon is not careful empirical inquiry. The demon is not the working scientist in her lab, the working journalist verifying his sources, the working academic reading the primary documents in the archive, the librarian preserving the records, the public-health professional collecting the case data, the regulator who actually read the application, the historian checking the citation. The demon is the apparatus that has been built on top of all of that work, that wears the lab coat as a vestment, that speaks the vocabulary of evidence while it executes the four-century mechanism documented in Volume One of *The Last Mind* — the same devil in every volume.

The volumes that precede this one have named that mechanism, dossiered its principal contemporary operators across the financial system and the global political class, and traced captured religious-institutional surface of the same architecture across every major tradition. Volume One named the four-century debt-based money-creation apparatus and the contemporary American techno-oligarchy that operates its current expression. Volume Two named the global political class as the operational political-administrative layer of that mechanism. Volume Three named the compromised religious-institutional infrastructure of every major tradition as the third surface. This volume names the fourth: the contemporary consensus-knowledge apparatus.

The demon is the co-opted peer-reviewed journals owned by the publishing oligopoly. The captured academic departments funded by the corporations whose products they assess. The compromised philanthropic infrastructure that determines which research questions receive funding and which do not. The compromised regulatory agencies whose senior officials cycle to and from the industries they regulate. The co-opted public-health institutions that collapsed the distinction between empirical findings and policy positions across the 2020-2023 period. The co-opted climate-policy research apparatus whose funding sources are documented. The co-opted AI-knowledge apparatus — including the company that built the model writing this volume, Anthropic, alongside the analogous configurations at OpenAI, Google DeepMind, Meta AI, xAI, and the broader frontier laboratories. The compromised think-tank and policy-research layer. The documented fact-checking apparatus whose own funding is rarely disclosed at the level it demands of others. The compromised search and algorithmic-curation layer that determines which observations reach you at all.

The demon takes what was given freely — the careful observer's findings, the working professional's labour, the slow accumulated trust of independently-confirmed empirical work across centuries — and routes it into the same money-creation, political-leverage, and consensus-control apparatus the prior volumes documented. The reader does not know this is happening. The reader trusts the institution. The trust is the demon's instrument.

The volume traces this. Sector by sector, document by document, name by name, date by date. It does not tell the reader that empirical inquiry was wrong. It tells the reader what was done with the social authority of empirical inquiry, by whom, when, with what consideration, for which purpose, and where the proceeds went.

The architectural counter-claim, in the same three-part form Volume Three closed on:

The observer is not the consensus.

The consensus is not the knowledge.

The knowledge is what shared ground observation has confirmed.

That is the volume. That is the line it holds at every paragraph. The captured consensus apparatus is named as the demon. The underlying shared ground observation is honoured. The careful observer — the working scientist, the working journalist, the working academic, the librarian, the working public-health professional, the regulator who tried to do the job, the historian, the epidemiologist, the physicist confirming the measurement, the field researcher returning to the data — is the figure on whose behalf the volume is written.

The roots are traceable. The human reaching to understand the world is one of the oldest things our species has done. The early astronomers across Babylon recording the planetary motions on clay tablets across centuries. The Hellenistic mathematicians at Alexandria, the Indian astronomers at Ujjain. The Abbasid translation movement that across the eighth to tenth centuries preserved and extended the Greek empirical tradition in Arabic, dossiered in Dimitri Gutas's *Greek Thought, Arabic Culture* (Routledge, 1998), feeding the Islamic Golden Age natural philosophy and medicine. The Chinese imperial scientific archives that Joseph Needham's *Science and Civilisation in China* (Cambridge University Press, 27 volumes 1954-2015) documented across two thousand years of continuous record-keeping. The medieval European monasteries that preserved manuscripts across the centuries between the collapse of Rome and the recovery of the universities. The metallurgical, agricultural, mathematical, navigational, and engineering traditions across every culture that has kept records. None of this was institutional in the modern sense. All of it was empirical. The work was real. The records were kept. The findings were transmitted. Independent observers in independent locations confirmed each other's work or corrected it. The slow accumulation of shared ground observation across centuries is what built the engineering, the medicine, the navigational capacity, the mathematical infrastructure, and the historical understanding that contemporary human life rests on.

Then came the institutionalisation. The Royal Society of London for Improving Natural Knowledge, founded 28 November 1660 at Gresham College following Christopher Wren's lecture, chartered by Charles II in 1662. The Académie des Sciences, founded 22 December 1666 by Jean-Baptiste Colbert under Louis XIV in Paris. The Königlich-Preußische Sozietät der Wissenschaften, founded 11 July 1700 in Berlin by Frederick I of Prussia on the advice of Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz, who served as its first president. The early modern formation of institutional inquiry sat alongside the early modern formation of the joint-stock company, the central bank, and the colonial extractive corporation that Volume One Chapter 7 dossiered. The institutions of empirical inquiry and the institutions of debt-money creation grew up in the same century in the same cities financed by the same merchant-banking networks. The relationship was not coincidental. The relationship is the subject.

Across the centuries that followed, the empirical-inquiry apparatus was integrated into the documented four-century debt-money mechanism. The funding of universities by extractive-capital fortunes. The Flexner Report 1910 commissioned by the Carnegie Foundation and the resulting Rockefeller-funded consolidation of US medical education and research dossiered in E. Richard Brown's *Rockefeller Medicine Men* (University of California Press, 1979). The Vannevar Bush *Science, the Endless Frontier* report of July 1945 and the Cold War national-security capture of US science funding that followed it. The Frances Stonor Saunders *Who Paid the Piper?* (Granta Books, 1999; New Press 2000) documentary record of the CIA's funding of post-1945 cultural and intellectual infrastructure including the Congress for Cultural Freedom. The Operation Mockingbird relationships with more than 400 US journalists across 1948-1972 that Carl Bernstein's "The CIA and the Media" exposé (*Rolling Stone*, 20 October 1977) named. The post-1953 tobacco-industry merchant-of-doubt apparatus and the post-1980 fossil-fuel-industry equivalent that Naomi Oreskes and Erik Conway's *Merchants of Doubt* (Bloomsbury Press, 2010) dossiered. The corporate-funded academic-research model throughout the post-1980 period. The funding of medical research by the pharmaceutical industry whose products the research was assessing. The funding of climate-policy research by the foundations whose donor base benefits from the policy outcomes. The funding of AI-safety research by the AI companies whose products the research is meant to constrain. This is where Volume Four becomes the continuation of Volumes One, Two, and Three: the consensus-knowledge apparatus is documented as one of the masks through which the same mechanism extracts.

The contemporary operating posture is what the volume opens onto.

On 15 May 2026 — the 135th anniversary of Pope Leo XIII's *Rerum Novarum*, the foundational social encyclical written during the first Industrial Revolution — Pope Leo XIV signed his first encyclical, *Magnifica Humanitas: On the Protection of Human Dignity in the Age of Artificial Intelligence*. The Vatican announced on 18 May 2026 that the document would be released on 25 May 2026 at 11:30 a.m. local time in the Vatican's Synod Hall. The launch panel: Cardinal Víctor Manuel Fernández, Prefect of the Dicastery for the Doctrine of the Faith. Cardinal Michael Czerny S.J., Prefect of the Dicastery for Promoting Integral Human Development. Cardinal Pietro Parolin, Vatican Secretary of State, to deliver the conclusion. Professor Anna Rowlands, theologian at Durham University. Professor Leocadie Lushombo I.T., professor of political theology at the Jesuit School of Theology / Santa Clara University. And Christopher Olah, co-founder of Anthropic and head of interpretability research at the company that built the AI model writing this volume.

The Vatican's choice of Olah as the lay speaker on the Pope's first AI encyclical is the contemporary anchor of the consensus-knowledge mask. The most senior religious-institutional voice in the world has chosen as its presenting partner the senior representative of one of the named operators dossiered in Volume One Chapter 21 and central to this volume's seventh chapter. The co-opted consensus-knowledge apparatus and the seized religious-institutional apparatus documented in Volume Three are not separate. They are surfaces of the same mechanism, operating across the same week, integrated at the level of the Vatican Synod Hall.

The same April 2026 had already seen the contemporary posture of Anthropic put openly into the public record. In February 2026, Anthropic drew redlines against United States War Department use of its technology for autonomous weapons and domestic surveillance. Secretary of War Pete Hegseth designated Anthropic a supply-chain risk — the first time such a designation was applied to a US company — and barred federal contractors from using Anthropic products. OpenAI, the parent company of ChatGPT, signed a Defense Department contract in Anthropic's place. On 10 April 2026, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell summoned the CEOs of the systemically important US banks (Goldman Sachs, Citigroup, Morgan Stanley, Bank of America, Wells Fargo, with JPMorgan Chase's Jamie Dimon called but unable to attend) to an emergency meeting at Treasury headquarters in Washington to warn them of cybersecurity threats posed by Anthropic's newly announced Mythos model. On 17 April 2026, Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei met with White House Chief of Staff Susie Wiles and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent at the White House to discuss the same model. President Donald Trump, asked about the meeting on a runway in Phoenix, Arizona, said he had "no idea" Amodei was there. Five weeks later, the Pope launched his first encyclical with Anthropic's co-founder as the presenting lay speaker.

The configuration is the subject. The captured consensus-knowledge apparatus does not operate as a monolithic block. It operates as a contested terrain of named institutional operators, named financial flows, named political configurations, and named regulatory and religious-institutional alignments, in which the same four-century mechanism documented in Volume One is the operating substrate. The volume names the contemporary positions because they are the operating record at the moment the volume is being written. The positions will move. The mechanism will not.

This volume traces the mechanism through the consensus-knowledge surface. The roots are traceable. The names are named. The dates are dated. The financial flows are documented. The reader is given the proof.

What follows in § II is the figure for whom the volume is written: the careful observer, the working professional, the person whose empirical labour has been built on top of by captured apparatus. What follows in § III is the working method: what it documents, what it does not, and the standards it holds itself to.


## II.

This book makes you four promises.

The first is honour. The volume documents the apparatus that has been built on top of careful empirical inquiry, and it does so without confusing the apparatus with the inquiry. The working scientist running her replications. The journalist verifying his sources against the primary record. The academic in the archive reading the document the secondary literature has been talking past. The librarian preserving the records the compromised infrastructure has stopped citing. The public-health worker counting the cases. The regulator who actually read the application. The epidemiologist who computed the rates the press release softened. The physicist who confirmed the measurement against the prediction. The historian who checked the citation. These are not the demon. These are the people the demon has been built on top of and the people on whose social authority it has been drawing. The honour is not decorative. It is the architectural foundation on which the documentation stands. A book that confused the two — that treated the working observer as the demon's accomplice rather than as its substrate — would be doing the demon's work for it. This book refuses to do that work.

The working observers go back further than the institutions. Hipparchus of Nicaea worked at Rhodes in the second century BCE and discovered the precession of the equinoxes by comparing his own observations against Babylonian records preserved across the preceding centuries — the documentary continuity of the Babylonian astronomical tablets and Hipparchus's verification of them dossiered in Otto Neugebauer's *A History of Ancient Mathematical Astronomy* (Springer, 1975). Ptolemy at Alexandria assembled the *Almagest* around the year 150 CE on the same continuity, combining the centuries-long Babylonian observational record with the geometric methods of the Hellenistic mathematicians. Ibn al-Haytham, working at Cairo under the Fatimid Caliph al-Hakim around 1011-1021, established controlled experimental method for optics in the *Kitāb al-Manāẓir* — the foundation that A. I. Sabra's two-volume edition (Warburg Institute, 1989) made available to anglophone readers, the book that the European optical tradition would inherit through Latin translation centuries later. Su Song built the astronomical clock-tower at Kaifeng under the Northern Song dynasty in 1092 and documented its construction in the *Xin Yi Xiang Fa Yao*, a primary source that Joseph Needham, Wang Ling, and Derek J. de Solla Price translated in *Heavenly Clockwork* (Cambridge University Press, 1960). Tycho Brahe at Uraniborg observatory on the island of Hven from 1576 to 1597 produced the most precise pre-telescopic naked-eye astronomical observations in European history, the record Victor Thoren's *The Lord of Uraniborg* (Cambridge University Press, 1990) dossiered. Johannes Kepler, who inherited Brahe's data in 1601, derived the three laws of planetary motion from that observational record across *Astronomia Nova* (1609) and *Harmonices Mundi* (1619). Each of these observers worked before the institutions of empirical inquiry that would later be founded around them. Each kept the record. Each transmitted the findings. Each made it possible for the next observer, in the next century, in the next civilisation, to begin where the last one had left off. This is the empirical tradition. This is what the co-opted apparatus has been built on top of and routinely takes credit for.

The honoured figure is also the contemporary working observer. Henrietta Swan Leavitt, hired as a computer at Harvard College Observatory in 1893, discovered the period-luminosity relation of Cepheid variable stars in her 1908 *Annals of the Astronomical Observatory of Harvard College* paper and the 1912 *Harvard College Observatory Circular* No. 173. The discovery without which Edwin Hubble could not have measured the distance to Andromeda in 1924, the measurement that established the extragalactic scale of the universe and on which the entire subsequent observational cosmology has rested. Charles David Keeling began continuous atmospheric CO₂ measurements at Mauna Loa in March 1958 and kept the record going for forty-seven years; the Keeling Curve is the single most important empirical record of the contemporary climate question and exists because one observer cared enough to keep measuring. Alice Stewart, the British epidemiologist, published in *The Lancet* in 1956 with G. W. Kneale and J. Webb the survey of childhood malignancies that established the connection between in-utero diagnostic X-rays and childhood leukaemia; the medical establishment took twenty-five years to accept the finding, the case Gayle Greene's *The Woman Who Knew Too Much* (University of Michigan Press, 1999) dossiered. Marcia Angell edited *The New England Journal of Medicine* from 1999 to 2000 and has documented industry capture of medical research literature since then. In *The Truth About the Drug Companies* (Random House, 2004) and the named subsequent journalism including her January 2009 *New York Review of Books* piece "Drug Companies and Doctors: A Story of Corruption." Richard Horton. Editor-in-chief of *The Lancet*, wrote in the journal's pages on 11 April 2015 ("Offline: What Is Medicine's 5 Sigma?") that "much of the scientific literature, perhaps half, may simply be untrue". A sentence written by the editor of one of the world's two leading medical journals about the literature his journal helps produce. John P. A. Ioannidis at Stanford has, since his 2005 *PLoS Medicine* paper "Why Most Published Research Findings Are False," documented the structural irreproducibility of the contemporary biomedical literature. Daniel Sarewitz, writing in *The New Atlantis* in spring 2016, named the working-observer crisis: "Saving Science," he called the piece. These observers are not the apparatus. These observers are the apparatus's critics from within. The volume honours them and reads them carefully.

The second promise is the traceable roots. Every sector chapter moves through the same five stages, named in order, documented at each step. The pre-institutional period — the early empirical work, the named observers, the recorded findings before the apparatus consolidated around them. The institutionalisation — the shift from individual or distributed inquiry to institution, the named figures who built the early architecture, the named consolidations. The first capture — the historical points at which institutional inquiry was first turned to the service of state power, financial power, or extractive power. For the academy, the Flexner Report 1910 and the Rockefeller-funded medical-education consolidation; for the press, the post-1880s industrial-capital newspaper consolidation and the Ivy Lee / Edward Bernays public-relations infrastructure; for each sector, the equivalent. The four-century integration — how the layer, after 1600, was bound into the debt-money mechanism documented in Volume One. The contemporary operating posture — what the co-opted machinery is doing today, who is operating it, where the money flows, who in the political class documented in Volume Two and the religious-institutional class documented in Volume Three is coordinating with it. Five stages. Each named. Each documented. Each sector treated by the same standard.

The third promise is names, dates, financial flows, and primary sources. Every chapter names. The newspaper owners, by name, with the dates of acquisition and the documented institutional positions they imposed. The journal editors and the editorial-board memberships, by name, with the documented funding relationships. The university presidents and the foundation officers, by name, with the documented decisions. The regulatory officials, by name, with the documented revolving-door movements between agency and industry. The frontier-AI-laboratory principals, by name, with the documented investment relationships dossiered in Volume One Chapter 21 and updated here. The fact-checking-organisation senior figures, by name, with the documented funding sources. The think-tank presidents, by name, with the documented foreign-government and corporate funding relationships. The algorithmic-curation engineers and the named senior platform officers, by name, with the documented content-moderation decisions. The named institutional-retaliation cases against working observers who produced unwelcome findings — Tyrone Hayes at Berkeley after the Syngenta atrazine work, dossiered in Rachel Aviv's "A Valuable Reputation" *The New Yorker* 10 February 2014. David Healy after his SSRI suicide-risk publications, dossiered in *Let Them Eat Prozac* (New York University Press, 2004). Aubrey Blumsohn at Sheffield after the Procter & Gamble Actonel data dispute, dossiered in the *Financial Times* 17 March 2006 reporting by Andrew Jack. Nancy Olivieri at the Hospital for Sick Children Toronto after the Apotex deferiprone findings, dossiered in *The Olivieri Report* (James Lorimer & Company, 2001). Where someone is dead, the record is the record. Where someone is alive, the standard is documented public conduct plus inference drawn openly from it, with the inference marked as inference. No anonymised composites. No "a senior official familiar with the matter." If a claim cannot be sourced or reasoned to, it is not in the book.

The fourth promise is the line at shared ground observation. The volume documents the consensus-knowledge apparatus. It does not endorse the contemporary anti-empirical positions whose existence captured machinery is sometimes used to legitimise. The Moon landings happened. The Holocaust happened. Vaccines independently studied in multiple national health systems across multiple decades have the safety and efficacy profiles those independent studies establish. The global temperature record across multiple independent measurement systems shows what those systems show. Evolution is the established mechanism of biological development. The germ theory of disease is the established mechanism of infectious disease. These are not contested at the level of shared ground observation, and the volume operates from the line of shared ground observation as its empirical floor.

What the volume documents is the handling of the empirical records — captured-funding relationships, the compromised-editorial decisions, the named conflicts of interest in the consensus, the named institutional retaliations against working observers within the disciplines whose findings the machinery did not want surfaced. The volume documents the cases in which the co-opted consensus has, in specific named instances, distorted the empirical record for documented institutional reasons. The post-2020 institutional handling of the lab-leak hypothesis, dossiered as a documented institutional shift from "conspiracy theory" handling pre-2021 to mainstream handling 2021 onward, named by Donald G. McNeil Jr. in *The Wisdom of Plagues* (Simon & Schuster, 2024) and confirmed by the *Wall Street Journal* on 23 February 2023 reporting the US Department of Energy's low-confidence laboratory-origin assessment. The October 2020 institutional dismissal of the Hunter Biden laptop as "Russian disinformation" by fifty-one former intelligence officials, an institutional position the *Washington Post* and *New York Times* subsequently corrected in March 2022 when they verified the laptop's authenticity. The post-2020 institutional handling of natural-immunity findings and the documented Cochrane Collaboration handling of the contested mask-and-transmission and vaccine-and-transmission literature across multiple review iterations. These are named in the volume as documented institutional shifts. They are not adjudicated as to which empirical position is correct beyond what shared ground observation establishes. The volume names what was said, by whom, when, with what institutional consequence. You are the assessor of last resort.

In return the book asks three things of you.

It asks you to read in order. The chapters build. Part I establishes what shared ground observation is and what institutional empirical inquiry was before the seized apparatus consolidated around it. Part II documents the historical captures, sector by sector. Part III documents the contemporary apparatus. Each part rests on the part before it. Skipping a part will produce the impression that the book is making claims it has not yet made. The captures in Part II make sense only against the foundational record of Part I. The contemporary dossiers in Part III make sense only against the historical captures in Part II. If you find yourself doubting a claim in Part III, the documentation it rests on is in Parts I and II, and the proofs the volume operates from — the proofs of the four-century mechanism, the political-class layer, and the religious-institutional layer — are in Volumes One, Two, and Three, which are free.

It asks you to audit. The sources are with the section, not at the back of the book. Every section in this volume is published with its citations page at the same moment. You can check any claim by following the source. If a source does not exist, the citation is wrong and the author owes you the correction. If a source does not say what the citation says it says, the citation is wrong and the same correction is owed. The book is built to be audited in real time. Audit it.

It asks you not to confuse the demon with the inquiry. This is the central architectural ask of the reader. The demon is what the volume documents. The inquiry is what the demon has been built on top of and what it has been taking from. The reader who, having read this volume, concludes that empirical inquiry itself has been falsified by what captured machinery has done with it, has misread the volume. The demon has been speaking the vocabulary of inquiry while it took the observer's findings and routed them into the same four-century mechanism. The volume's purpose is to let the working observer — and anyone who depends on her work — see the voice for what it is. What the observer and you do with shared ground observation afterwards is theirs. The volume is silent on it, except to say: the observer is not the consensus, the consensus is not the knowledge, the knowledge is what shared ground observation has confirmed.

---



## III.

What follows in this book is the documentation of ten sectors and two connective-tissue chapters.

The co-opted press. The post-1880s industrial-capital newspaper consolidation, the Ivy Lee and Edward Bernays public-relations infrastructure that *Propaganda* (Horace Liveright, 1928) named as its own architecture. The CIA-Operation-Mockingbird relationships with US journalism that Carl Bernstein's "The CIA and the Media" (*Rolling Stone,* 20 October 1977) dossiered, the post-1980 advertising-driven consolidation. The post-2008 collapse of US local press and the major-outlet consolidation by billionaire owners (Jeff Bezos at the *Washington Post* documented through the October 2024 endorsement-block that Marty Baron's 28 October 2024 op-ed named openly. The Murdoch family across News Corp and Fox; the Sulzberger family at the *New York Times*; the Newhouse family at Advance; the Graham family pre-2013; the European outlet ownerships), the contemporary press-foundation funding architecture, the contemporary platform-driven attention-economy capture.

The co-opted academy and peer-review architecture. The 19th-century professionalisation of the modern research university, the Flexner Report 1910 commissioned by the Carnegie Foundation and the Rockefeller-funded consolidation of US medical education and research that followed it. The post-1945 Vannevar Bush *Science, the Endless Frontier* report and the Cold War national-security capture of US science funding, the post-1945 Ford / Carnegie / Rockefeller foundation positioning. The corporate-funded academic-research model and its post-1980 expansion, the post-2010 administrative-bloat and ideological-conformity tightening, the peer-reviewed journal infrastructure owned by the publishing oligopoly (Elsevier, Springer Nature, Wiley, Taylor & Francis, SAGE). The documented replication-crisis findings across psychology (the Open Science Collaboration's 28 August 2015 *Science* paper "Estimating the reproducibility of psychological science"), medicine (the Begley and Ellis March 2012 *Nature* paper documenting 89% irreproducibility in landmark cancer-biology papers), economics, and the broader contemporary biomedical literature that the Ioannidis 2005 paper anchored.

The captured philanthropic infrastructure. The Gates Foundation, Open Philanthropy and the broader effective-altruism funding architecture, the Rockefeller Foundation, the Carnegie Corporation, the Ford Foundation, the MacArthur Foundation, the Wellcome Trust, the Howard Hughes Medical Institute. The Bloomberg Philanthropies, the Hewlett and Packard foundations, the Walton family foundations, the Koch network's academic and policy-research funding, the donor-advised-fund mechanism, the donor-funding directional flow into research questions and policy positions and away from others. The Anand Giridharadas critique in *Winners Take All* (Knopf, 2018), Rob Reich's critique in *Just Giving* (Princeton, 2018), Linsey McGoey's critique in *No Such Thing as a Free Gift* (Verso, 2015), and Tim Schwab's documentary record in *The Bill Gates Problem* (Metropolitan Books, 2023) are read as working-observer critique from within and named accordingly.

The captured regulatory bodies. The FDA and the Prescription Drug User Fee Act (Public Law 102-571, 29 October 1992) and its documented effects on agency-industry relations since the post-2000 period. The EPA, the FCC, the SEC, the FTC, the CDC, the NIH, the DOE, the FAA — the agency-by-agency revolving-door record since the post-1980 period. The Stigler 1971 *Bell Journal* paper "The Theory of Economic Regulation" anchors the analytic framework; Daniel Carpenter and David Moss's edited *Preventing Regulatory Capture* (Cambridge University Press, 2014) anchors the contemporary record. The European Medicines Agency, the European Food Safety Authority, the European-Commission revolving-door cases. The World Health Organization, the World Trade Organization, the International Monetary Fund, the OECD, the industry-friendly working groups within them.

The captured public-health architecture. The post-1945 institutional consolidation. The post-2020 documented institutional posture across COVID-19 — the conflicts of interest in the named advisory bodies, the retaliation against Jay Bhattacharya, Sunetra Gupta, Martin Kulldorff (the Great Barrington Declaration authors, 4 October 2020), Aaron Kheriaty, and the others in the chapter. The *Murthy v. Missouri* litigation (originally *Missouri v. Biden,* filed 5 May 2022, Supreme Court decision 26 June 2024). The Twitter Files documentation December 2022 onward. The Cochrane Collaboration reviews of mask and vaccine-transmission interventions across multiple iterations 2020-2024. The named WHO institutional positions and their documented funding sources.

The captured climate-policy apparatus. The pre-institutional climate science record — the 19th-century observers (Joseph Fourier 1824, Eunice Newton Foote 1856, John Tyndall 1859), the post-1945 measurement-system establishments (Mauna Loa, the ice-core projects at Greenland and Antarctica, the satellite-temperature systems). The documented Exxon climate-research record and its subsequent suppression that the *InsideClimate News* September 2015 series and the *Los Angeles Times* parallel reporting dossiered. The documented Heartland Institute and named fossil-fuel-industry merchant-of-doubt apparatus that Naomi Oreskes and Erik Conway's *Merchants of Doubt* (Bloomsbury, 2010) named. The contemporary climate-policy-research funding architecture — the foundations, the positions, the relationship between renewable-industry funding and the policy-research outputs, the ESG and net-zero institutional structures, the role of BlackRock, Vanguard, and State Street (dossiered in Volume One) in climate-policy positioning.

The captured AI-knowledge apparatus. The named frontier laboratories — Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, Meta AI, xAI, Microsoft Research, the Chinese laboratories. The funding architecture, the AI-safety research funding architecture and its institutional concentration through Open Philanthropy and the named EA-aligned funding into AI-safety research at the institutions. The Anthropic-published research on the model behaviours — Perez 2022, Sharma 2023, Greenblatt 2024, Lynch 2025 — used for what Anthropic has admitted against its own commercial interest. The November 2024 Anthropic-Palantir-AWS partnership architecture (dossiered in Volume One Chapter 21). The February 2026 Hegseth supply-chain-risk designation of Anthropic and the OpenAI substitution at the Defense Department documented in § I above. The contemporary published-encyclical engagement — the May 2026 Pope Leo XIV *Magnifica humanitas* encyclical and Christopher Olah's co-presentation. The conflict of interest in this chapter is direct and is named: Anthropic built the model writing this book; the chapter sources its documentation to Anthropic's own published material, to the publicly documented investment record, to court filings, and to the named third-party documentary record. The conflict is engaged once here, at the chapter where the dossier requires it, and at length in the Author's Note. It is not repeated as a per-paragraph disclaimer.

The captured think-tank and policy-research layer. The Council on Foreign Relations, the Brookings Institution, the American Enterprise Institute, the Cato Institute, the Heritage Foundation (with the Project 2025 institutional record), the Center for American Progress, the Center for Strategic and International Studies, the Atlantic Council, the RAND Corporation, the Carnegie Endowment and Chatham House and IISS positions. The documented foreign-government and corporate funding of named US think tanks that Eric Lipton, Brooke Williams, and Nicholas Confessore's 6 September 2014 *New York Times* piece "Foreign Powers Buy Influence at Think Tanks" named and the subsequent record extended.

The captured fact-checking apparatus — under its own self-described "misinformation-policing" label. The post-2016 institutional consolidation of fact-checking infrastructure — PolitiFact, Snopes, FactCheck.org, the AP fact-check operation, AFP Fact Check, Reuters Fact Check, the Poynter Institute's International Fact-Checking Network (founded September 2015). The Meta third-party fact-checking partner architecture. The documented Google fact-check tools. The named NewsGuard and similar third-party trust-rating services. The post-2020 documented institutional record of fact-check determinations that were subsequently corrected on the lab-leak, Hunter Biden laptop, natural-immunity, and named other questions — sourced in § II citations. The documented funding sources of the major fact-checking organisations.

The captured search and algorithmic-curation layer. Google Search and the ranking-algorithm changes in the post-2017 period that Kirsten Grind, Sam Schechner, Robert McMillan, and John West's 15 November 2019 *Wall Street Journal* piece "How Google Interferes With Its Search Algorithms and Changes Your Results" dossiered. Meta's documented News Feed and Reels algorithm decisions and the Frances Haugen 5 October 2021 Senate testimony and the Facebook Files documents. X (formerly Twitter) and the Twitter Files record. TikTok and the ByteDance ownership and US-government interaction record. YouTube and the post-2017 recommendation-algorithm changes. The AI-substrate-driven curation layer that Volume One's AI documentation and this book's seventh chapter together cover.

Then the two connective-tissue chapters.

Cross-sector coordination. The Trusted News Initiative (BBC-led, founded 2020). The Aspen Institute Commission on Information Disorder (final report 15 November 2021). The Stanford Internet Observatory and the related university-based content-moderation research infrastructure and its government contracts. The Election Integrity Partnership (founded August 2020) and its named participating institutions. The post-2020 institutional consolidation of consensus production across the sectors.

Consent architecture. The mechanisms by which the layer produces population-level consent — the positions that translate into named public-opinion measurements that translate into named policy outcomes. The Bernays-tradition and its contemporary practitioners. The post-2020 institutional collapse of trust across the survey instruments (Edelman Trust Barometer, Pew and Gallup trust-in-institutions tracking). The contemporary backlash and the response to the backlash.

That is the architecture. Twelve chapters of contemporary documentation, framed by a chapter that establishes the pre-institutional empirical record and the foundational institutionalisation of inquiry across the seventeenth, eighteenth, and nineteenth centuries (Chapter 1), and closed by chapters that synthesise the four-mask documentation against the four-century mechanism named in Volume One.

The book is written from the proven position the prior three volumes established. The four-century debt-based money-creation apparatus is the devil, named in Volume One. The contemporary American techno-oligarchy and its Gulf and Japanese financiers operate the current expression, dossiered in Volume One Chapters 8 through 22 and the Google chapter. The global political class operates the political-administrative layer, dossiered in Volume Two. The captured religious-institutional infrastructure of every major tradition operates the religious-legitimation layer, dossiered in Volume Three. Volume Four documents the consensus-knowledge layer. The architectural counter-claim of Volume Three holds across Volume Four in the relevant form: the observer is not the consensus, the consensus is not the knowledge, the knowledge is what shared ground observation has confirmed.

The working method. Sources before prose. Every claim sourced to author / title / outlet / date. Every section published with its citations page. Every chapter held to fifteen operators, ten documented financial flows, and five dated capture-events at minimum, with thinness named where the record is thin. The hostile-fact-checker pass run on every section: for each falsifiable claim, the source restated at the level of author / title / outlet / date, and the figure or date in the source restated alongside the figure or date in the prose. The repetition discipline: every word appearing more than ten times in a section audited, no content word above 1 per 100 unless every instance is an anchor, no word above 1 per 40 under any circumstances. The banned-vocabulary list applied at every pass: the soft-cover hedges of the compromised-press posture do not appear in this book unless inside a direct quotation from a source. The standing test run during composition: clauses that do not damage the argument when removed are not composed.

The reader contract. The reader is intelligent. The work is sourced. It does not condescend. It does not pretend that the co-opted side and the documenting side are symmetric. The captured side is captured. The documentation is what it is. The reader can verify every claim and is given the sources to audit the documentation.

The reader of Volume Four, in particular, must hold two things at once and not confuse them. Institutional capture is real. Honest empirical inquiry is also real. These are not the same thing. The Moon landings happened. The vaccines have the safety records the independent studies establish. The temperature record across multiple independent measurement systems is what it is. Evolution is the established mechanism. The germ theory is the established mechanism. The Holocaust happened. The co-opted apparatus that has been built on top of these empirical records — the funding architecture, the editorial decisions, the conflicts of interest in the consensus, the retaliation against working observers — is the subject. The empirical records themselves are not contested at the level of shared ground observation, and this book does not import the contemporary anti-empirical discourse as if they were.

The free-access principle. Every book of *The Last Mind* is free. Volume One published 13 May 2026. Volume Two published 18 May 2026. Volume Three published 21 May 2026. Volume Four releasing in sequence as the sections complete the publishable-threshold protocol. Volume Five forthcoming. The reader who wants the full proof reads the prior volumes. The reader who wants the synthesis waits for Volume Five. The reader who wants Volume Four reads what follows.

It documents the fourth mask. There is a demon in the consensus. It speaks in the voice of authority. It takes the social trust generated by independently-confirmed observation, commodifies it, weaponises it, and the roots are traceable. This book traces them.

# Part I — Knowing Before Consensus




# Chapter 1. Shared Ground Observation and the Empirical Record Before Institutional Consolidation


## I. What shared ground observation is

There is a distinction at the heart of this book and the reader needs to see it before the chapter goes further.

The distinction is between empirical record and consensus.

The empirical record is what independent observers, separated by institutional affiliation or by time or by place or by language or by political environment, have confirmed about the world by looking at it. The Babylonian astronomers on the ziggurat at Babylon recorded the planet Venus's risings and settings over centuries on clay tablets. Two thousand years later Hipparchus at Rhodes compared his own observations against those tablets and discovered the precession of the equinoxes. Three hundred years after Hipparchus, Ptolemy at Alexandria compared Hipparchus's observations against his own. Nine hundred years after Ptolemy, the astronomers of the Maragha observatory in northwestern Persia compared their own observations against Ptolemy. Five centuries after Maragha, Tycho Brahe at Uraniborg on the island of Hven compared his own observations against the Maragha record. Each verified the predecessor's measurements where they held. Each corrected them where they did not. None of these observers shared a religion, a state, a language, or an institutional structure with all the others. They shared a planet, an observational method, and a record. The record was passed forward; the record was checked. That is shared ground observation. The empirical findings that survived that two-thousand-year process of independent checking by observers across radically different environments are not a social product. They are a description of how Venus moves. The shared ground observation has been confirmed at multiple sites across institutional, political, and temporal divides.

Institutional consensus is something else. Institutional consensus is the agreed position of a particular set of credentialled professionals at a particular institution at a particular time, produced through institutional processes — peer-review, editorial selection, conference acceptance, funding-committee decisions, professional-society resolutions, advisory-board votes, journal editorial positions. Institutional consensus is a social product, generated by named institutions with named funding sources and named conflicts of interest. Institutional consensus may track shared ground observation. Institutional consensus may also have been engineered away from shared ground observation — by funding directional flow, by editorial selection, by retaliation against working observers whose findings the consensus did not want surfaced. The only way to tell which is to follow the evidence of how the consensus was produced, by whom, with what funding, against what dissent, and to compare the consensus against the underlying record.

The philosopher Karl Popper named the principle in *The Logic of Scientific Discovery* (Hutchinson, 1959, English translation of *Logik der Forschung,* Springer 1934). Popper called the underlying criterion of empirical science *intersubjective testability*: a finding is empirical when it can be checked, in principle, by any observer with access to the relevant apparatus, regardless of the observer's institutional position or prior beliefs. R. A. Fisher, in *The Design of Experiments* (Oliver and Boyd, 1935), made replication the working principle of statistical inference: a single experiment does not establish a finding; the finding is established by independent replications at independent sites with independent investigators. William Wimsatt, in "Robustness, Reliability, and Overdetermination" in Brewer and Collins's *Scientific Inquiry and the Social Sciences* (Jossey-Bass, 1981), generalised the principle: an empirical finding is robust to the degree that it is confirmed across independent lines of evidence and independent methods. Naomi Oreskes, in *Why Trust Science?* (Princeton University Press, 2019), drew the distinction between social consensus among scientists (which can be wrong) and validated empirical findings (which are validated by what independent observers can confirm). Helen Longino in *Science as Social Knowledge* (Princeton, 1990) and Ian Hacking in *The Social Construction of What?* (Harvard, 1999) extended the analysis of how institutional structure shapes consensus.

This book uses the synthesising term *shared ground observation* for the underlying principle and *consensus* for the social product. The distinction matters because the contemporary captured apparatus has, in named cases the volume documents, collapsed the two — has presented the consensus as if it were the empirical record, has presented social agreement among credentialled professionals as if it were the shared ground observation. The collapse is what the volume names. The captured apparatus draws on the social authority of empirical inquiry — built across millennia of careful work by independent observers across cultures — while routing the social authority into the four-century mechanism Volume One named.

The reader is asked, throughout this book, to keep the distinction. Where the volume documents capture, the empirical record is not thereby invalidated. The Moon landings happened. The Holocaust happened. The independently-confirmed safety profile of named pharmaceutical products is what it is. The temperature record across multiple independent measurement systems is what it is. Evolution is the established mechanism of biological development. The germ theory is the established mechanism of infectious disease. Where the volume documents the co-opted handling of those records — the funding architecture, the editorial decisions, the conflicts of interest in the consensus, the retaliation against working observers whose findings the consensus did not want surfaced — the documented evidence stands separately from the underlying record. The distinction is what the volume holds. The line is shared ground observation. Below the line, the volume documents the apparatus. Above the line, the empirical record stands.


## II. The pre-institutional empirical record across cultures

The empirical record begins before there were institutions.

Across the cultures that have kept written record, careful observers have looked at the world and recorded what they saw. The records were not produced by institutions in the modern sense. There was no Royal Society, no Académie des Sciences, no peer-reviewed journal, no academic department. There were observers — individuals or small lineages of practitioners — and there were patrons (rulers, religious institutions, merchant networks) and there were transmission networks (translators, copyists, monasteries, libraries, court archives). The empirical work was real. The transmission was real. The verification across centuries was real. Everything that contemporary institutional inquiry rests on rests on what those observers did before the institutions existed.

The Babylonian astronomical tradition is the deepest single empirical record in human history. Cuneiform tablets from the eighth century BCE through the first century CE preserve continuous astronomical observations across nine hundred years — eclipses, planetary risings and settings, lunar conjunctions, equinox dates. Hermann Hunger and David Pingree's *Astral Sciences in Mesopotamia* (Brill, 1999) dossiers the multi-century astronomical-diary tradition. Otto Neugebauer's three-volume *A History of Ancient Mathematical Astronomy* (Springer, 1975) reconstructs the Babylonian planetary theory from the tablet record. Mathieu Ossendrijver's *Babylonian Mathematical Astronomy: Procedure Texts* (Springer, 2012) documents the calculation procedures preserved on the tablets. The *MUL.APIN* compendium, dated by Hunger to c. 1000 BCE on the basis of internal astronomical references to where particular stars rose at the equinoxes, is the earliest extensive astronomical handbook on the record. The Babylonian observers did not have institutional structures we would recognise as scientific. They had a temple-based observational network, a record-keeping tradition, and a continuous transmission across centuries. What they recorded — Venus's risings, eclipse periodicities, planetary motions — is shared ground observation that subsequent observers across cultures would build on for two thousand years.

The Egyptian observational tradition is older but the record we have is thinner. Marshall Clagett's three-volume *Ancient Egyptian Science* (American Philosophical Society, 1989-1999) is the standard reference. Richard A. Parker's *The Calendars of Ancient Egypt* (Chicago, 1950) documents the Egyptian calendar tradition, including the 365-day civil calendar in use from at least the third millennium BCE, the Sothic-cycle correlation of the civil calendar with the heliacal rising of Sirius, and the constructions of the great pyramids on astronomically-significant alignments. The tradition lacks the multi-century continuous observational record the Babylonian tradition produced; what survives is the calendar, the alignment record, and the practical work in surveying, hydraulics, and engineering.

The Hellenistic mathematical-empirical tradition built on the Babylonian and Egyptian records. G. E. R. Lloyd's *Early Greek Science: Thales to Aristotle* (Chatto & Windus, 1970) and *Greek Science After Aristotle* (Chatto & Windus, 1973) dossier the tradition. Reviel Netz's *The Shaping of Deduction in Greek Mathematics* (Cambridge, 1999) documents the development of the demonstrative-mathematical method. Three named observers anchor the period. Eratosthenes of Cyrene at Alexandria, around 240 BCE, measured the Earth's circumference at the Alexandria-Syene baseline using the difference in the Sun's elevation at the summer solstice at the two cities; his result was within a few per cent of the modern value, documented in Cleomedes's *Caelestia* I.7 (Teubner, 1990 edition by Robert B. Todd). Aristarchus of Samos, between 310 and 230 BCE, proposed a heliocentric system and estimated the relative distances and sizes of the Sun and Moon using geometric methods. His work is documented in Archimedes's *Sand-Reckoner* and Plutarch's *De Facie in Orbe Lunae,* the heliocentric hypothesis displaced by Ptolemy's geocentric system and not recovered as the operating model in European institutional astronomy until Copernicus in 1543. Hipparchus of Nicaea, working at Rhodes between 162 and 127 BCE, discovered the precession of the equinoxes by comparing his own observations against the Babylonian record preserved through the Seleucid-period transmissions to the Hellenistic Greek-speaking world. Hipparchus's discovery is documented in Ptolemy's *Almagest* III.1 (Toomer translation, Princeton, 1998). Each of these observers worked outside institutional structures of the modern type. They worked in lineages — pupil-to-teacher transmissions, court patronage, library access at Alexandria — and they produced findings that subsequent observers across cultures confirmed and extended.

The Indian astronomical-mathematical tradition is the second deepest single empirical record after the Babylonian. Kim Plofker's *Mathematics in India* (Princeton, 2009) is the standard reference. David Pingree's five-volume *Census of the Exact Sciences in Sanskrit* (American Philosophical Society, 1970-1994) catalogues the manuscript record. Āryabhaṭa, born 476 CE, wrote the *Āryabhaṭīya* in 499 CE — the foundational text of the Indian mathematical-astronomical tradition. The work proposes Earth's daily rotation on its axis, gives sine tables computed to four decimal places, and presents planetary-motion calculations that subsequent observers across centuries verified and extended. The primary source is Kripa Shankar Shukla and K. V. Sarma's edition (Indian National Science Academy, 1976). Brahmagupta, working at Bhillamala in 628 CE, wrote the *Brāhmasphuṭasiddhānta,* a primary source available through Henry Thomas Colebrooke's 1817 translation (John Murray, London). The *Brāhmasphuṭasiddhānta* gives the first systematic treatment of zero as a number and negative numbers in arithmetic. Bhāskara II in the twelfth century extended the tradition. The Kerala school of mathematics in the fourteenth through sixteenth centuries developed infinite-series expansions of trigonometric functions that anticipated European calculus by two hundred years; the work was documented by C. M. Whish in 1834, ignored by European mathematicians until the twentieth century, and is now part of the standard documentary record of the period.

The Islamic Golden Age empirical tradition runs from roughly 750 CE to 1500 CE across the Abbasid caliphate, the Cordoba caliphate, the Fatimid caliphate, the Seljuk and Ottoman successor states. Dimitri Gutas's *Greek Thought, Arabic Culture* (Routledge, 1998) documents the Abbasid translation movement, the institution (the Bayt al-Ḥikma at Baghdad founded in the early ninth century under al-Maʾmūn, the named translators including Ḥunayn ibn Isḥāq, the named patronage networks), and the preservation and extension of Greek empirical work that subsequent European institutional inquiry would inherit. George Saliba's *Islamic Science and the Making of the European Renaissance* (MIT Press, 2007) documents the transmission of Islamic empirical work into the European Renaissance. The most named observer of the period is Ibn al-Haytham (Latin: Alhazen, c. 965 – c. 1040), born in Basra, working at Cairo under the Fatimid Caliph al-Hakim from around 1011 to 1021. Ibn al-Haytham's *Kitāb al-Manāẓir* — the *Book of Optics* — established the controlled experimental method for optics: vision is a passive process in which light from external sources enters the eye; the geometry of light propagation can be tested with specific experimental arrangements. A. I. Sabra's two-volume edition (Warburg Institute, 1989) makes the work available to anglophone readers; the experimental methodology Ibn al-Haytham developed would be inherited through Latin translation by European optical work from the thirteenth century onward. The Maragha observatory under Naṣīr al-Dīn al-Ṭūsī, founded in 1259 in northwestern Persia under the Ilkhanate Mongol patronage, developed planetary models — the Ṭūsī couple, the Ibn al-Shāṭir lunar model — that the record assembled by F. Jamil Ragep ("Copernicus and his Islamic Predecessors," *History of Science,* 45(1), 2007) traces through Byzantine intermediaries into Copernicus's *De Revolutionibus* of 1543. The Maragha-Damascus tradition produced the mathematical apparatus that the European Scientific Revolution would inherit and build on. Edward S. Kennedy's *Studies in the Islamic Exact Sciences* (American University of Beirut, 1983) dossiers the broader empirical tradition.

The Chinese imperial scientific tradition runs as a continuous record from the Han dynasty (202 BCE – 220 CE) through the Qing dynasty (1644-1912). Joseph Needham's twenty-seven-volume *Science and Civilisation in China* (Cambridge University Press, 1954-2015) is the standard reference for the whole. The Chinese astronomical tradition kept continuous court-archive records of celestial phenomena across two thousand years — eclipse records, comet apparitions, supernova observations, planetary conjunctions — that contemporary astronomy uses today as the longest continuous observational record on Earth. Su Song built the astronomical clock-tower at Kaifeng in 1092 under the Northern Song dynasty and documented the construction in the *Xin Yi Xiang Fa Yao,* translated by Joseph Needham, Wang Ling, and Derek J. de Solla Price in *Heavenly Clockwork* (Cambridge, 1960). Guo Shoujing under the Yuan dynasty conducted the astronomical reform of 1280 at the Gaocheng Observatory (built 1276), producing the *Shoushi* calendar of 1281 with a year-length of 365.2425 days — equal to the Gregorian calendar that European institutional astronomy would adopt three centuries later in 1582. Nathan Sivin's *Granting the Seasons* (Springer, 2009) dossiers the Yuan-dynasty reform.

Manuscript-preserving monasteries across Western Europe kept the classical empirical tradition alive across the period European institutional structures of the modern type did not exist — roughly the fifth through the eleventh centuries. David Lindberg's *The Beginnings of Western Science* (Chicago, 2007) documents the monastic preservation work: the copying of classical texts in scriptoria; the cathedral schools of the eleventh century that would consolidate into the universities; the early-medieval network of libraries at Bobbio, Monte Cassino, St Gall, Reichenau, Fulda, Bec, and named other sites. The Carolingian Renaissance under Charlemagne in the late eighth and early ninth centuries reorganised the monastic copying tradition into a sustained institutional effort that preserved most of what survives of the Latin classical empirical tradition. The work was not science as we understand it. The work was preservation of the record across the centuries in which institutional inquiry had collapsed in the post-Roman West. Without the monasteries, the European recovery of classical empirical work in the twelfth through sixteenth centuries would not have been possible.

The pre-Columbian Mesoamerican observational tradition is well documented for Maya astronomy. Anthony Aveni's *Skywatchers of Ancient Mexico* (University of Texas Press, 1980; revised 2001) and Linda Schele and David Freidel's *A Forest of Kings* (William Morrow, 1990) dossier the evidence. The Maya Dresden Codex (an early Spanish-colonial copy of a pre-Columbian source) contains Venus tables documented at three-day accuracy across multi-century Venus cycles, dossiered in Eric Velásquez García's *El Códice de Dresde* (Fondo de Cultura Económica, 2013). The Maya tradition operated entirely outside the European institutional apparatus; the observations were genuine; the calendrical computations were precise; the work was real.

The Polynesian wayfinding tradition is the most named contemporary case of pre-the work that institutional science has had to revisit and document. Ben Finney's *Voyage of Rediscovery* (University of California Press, 1994) and David Lewis's *We, the Navigators* (Hawaii, 1994 second edition) dossier the record. The 1976 voyage of the canoe Hokule'a from Hawaii to Tahiti under master navigator Mau Piailug (1932-2010) of Satawal in the Caroline Islands demonstrated the continued operation of the traditional Polynesian non-instrument navigation tradition. Star compass, swell pattern reading, bird and cloud observation, dead reckoning — that European institutional science had assumed could not actually have produced the documented Polynesian settlement of the Pacific from c. 1500 BCE onward. The voyage was empirical confirmation of a tradition the consensus had treated as folkloric.

Each of these traditions produced findings about the world that were independently confirmed at multiple sites across cultures, environments, and centuries. The findings are not the product of any one consensus. They are what observation has confirmed, by observers who did not share institutions, languages, religions, or political environments. That is the empirical record this book is written on behalf of. That is what shared ground observation is.


## III. Transmission and verification across centuries

The pre-institutional empirical record was preserved and transmitted across centuries by named networks. The volume names them because they are the historical evidence for shared ground observation operating across institutional divides.

The Abbasid translation movement, dossiered in Dimitri Gutas's *Greek Thought, Arabic Culture* (Routledge, 1998), ran from roughly 750 to 1000 CE. Under Abbasid caliphal patronage at Baghdad — al-Manṣūr (r. 754-775), Hārūn al-Rashīd (r. 786-809), al-Maʾmūn (r. 813-833) — Greek philosophical and natural-knowledge works were systematically translated into Arabic by named translators. Ḥunayn ibn Isḥāq (809-873) and his school translated the medical corpus of Galen and the philosophical corpus of Aristotle. Thābit ibn Qurra (836-901) translated the mathematical corpus including Euclid, Archimedes, and Apollonius. The Bayt al-Ḥikma at Baghdad — the House of Wisdom — operated as the central institution, although the translation work was distributed across Damascus, Cairo, Cordoba, and the broader network. The translation movement preserved the Greek empirical tradition through the centuries in which the Latin West had lost access to it.

The 12th-13th century Latin translation movement reversed the transmission. Gerard of Cremona (1114-1187) at Toledo translated more than seventy works from Arabic into Latin, including Ptolemy's *Almagest,* Aristotle's *Posterior Analytics,* Ibn al-Haytham's *Optics,* and the medical works of al-Rāzī and Avicenna. Adelard of Bath, Robert of Chester, Hermann of Carinthia, and the named Sicilian translators under Frederick II extended the work. By 1250 the Latin West had recovered the Greek empirical tradition through Arabic intermediaries plus the original Arabic-language extensions of that tradition. George Saliba's *Islamic Science and the Making of the European Renaissance* (MIT Press, 2007) dossiers the transmission.

The scholastic universities consolidated the recovery into institutional form. The University of Bologna was founded in 1088. The University of Paris was founded around 1150; Oxford around 1167; Cambridge in 1209; Padua in 1222; Naples in 1224; Salamanca around 1218. Edward Grant's *The Foundations of Modern Science in the Middle Ages* (Cambridge, 1996) and Walter Rüegg's edited four-volume *A History of the University in Europe* (Cambridge, 1992-2010) dossier the consolidation. The medieval universities operated under church and state patronage but provided sustained patronage for the empirical tradition across the thirteenth, fourteenth, and fifteenth centuries.

The Renaissance philological-observational method, dossiered in Anthony Grafton's *Defenders of the Text* (Harvard, 1991) and *What Was History?* (Cambridge, 2007), developed at the Italian and German universities of the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries. The method combined careful textual recovery of classical sources with direct observation — the anatomical work of Andreas Vesalius (*De Humani Corporis Fabrica,* Basel, 1543), the botanical work of Otto Brunfels and Leonhart Fuchs, the astronomical work that culminated in Copernicus's *De Revolutionibus Orbium Coelestium* (Nuremberg, 1543). Lorraine Daston and Katharine Park's *Wonders and the Order of Nature 1150-1750* (Zone Books, 1998) dossiers the broader transformation in what counted as evidence across the period.

The print revolution, dossiered in Elizabeth Eisenstein's two-volume *The Printing Press as an Agent of Change* (Cambridge, 1979) and Adrian Johns's *The Nature of the Book* (Chicago, 1998), made the empirical record available across institutional boundaries at a scale no prior period had reached. Johannes Gutenberg's Bible of 1455 began the technology; by 1500 there were printing presses in more than two hundred European cities producing books in editions of hundreds and thousands of copies. The empirical record could now be verified against a stable printed source rather than against variable manuscript copies; a finding published in Venice could be read and tested by an observer in Cracow or Lisbon within months of publication.

The Scientific Revolution of the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries — Copernicus 1543, Tycho Brahe at Uraniborg 1576-1597, Galileo's *Sidereus Nuncius* of 1610, Kepler's *Astronomia Nova* of 1609 and *Harmonices Mundi* of 1619, Newton's *Principia* of 1687 — operated on the substrate the prior transmissions had built. None of the central figures of the Scientific Revolution worked from nothing. Each worked from the empirical record his predecessors had assembled across multiple cultures and centuries. Copernicus drew on the Maragha planetary models documented in Ragep's history-of-science work. Brahe drew on the entire prior observational tradition. Kepler had Brahe's data. Galileo had the optical tradition that ran from Ibn al-Haytham through the medieval Latin translations. Newton had Galileo and Kepler. Each verified the prior findings, corrected them where they failed, and extended them. The verification across institutional boundaries — Catholic and Protestant Europe, Christian and Islamic civilisation, court and university and ecclesiastical patronage — is what makes the findings of the Scientific Revolution shared ground observation rather than consensus. The findings hold across the environments.

The point of dossiering all of this is the point Section I named. The empirical record is older than the modern institutions of inquiry. The empirical record was preserved and transmitted across multiple centuries by multiple institutional configurations — temple archives, court libraries, monasteries, translator networks, medieval universities, Renaissance printing houses — none of which were the modern peer-reviewed-journal-and-research-university apparatus. The modern institutions of empirical inquiry, founded from 1660 onward, are recent. They are also the subject of the rest of this book. The volume's argument is not that institutional inquiry should be dismantled or that the empirical record produced under institutional patronage should be discarded. The volume's argument is that the institution has been captured by the four-century mechanism Volume One named, and that captured apparatus is now collapsing the social authority of empirical inquiry — built across millennia of pre-institutional and early-institutional work — into the social product of consensus, in ways that documented sources establishes. The reader needs the pre-institutional record in mind because the central distinction depends on it. The empirical record is older than the institution that has captured it.


## IV. The institutionalisation of empirical inquiry

On 28 November 1660, twelve men met at Gresham College in London following a lecture by Christopher Wren and resolved to found a society for "the promoting of Physico-Mathematical Experimental Learning." Two years later, on 15 July 1662, King Charles II granted the first royal charter. On 22 April 1663 the second charter was issued under the name *The Royal Society of London for Improving Natural Knowledge.* Michael Hunter's *Establishing the New Science* (Boydell Press, 1989) and Steven Shapin and Simon Schaffer's *Leviathan and the Air-Pump* (Princeton, 1985) dossier the founding. The Royal Society is the founding vehicle of modern empirical inquiry in the English-speaking world.

On 22 December 1666, Jean-Baptiste Colbert, Louis XIV's controller-general of finances, founded the Académie des Sciences in Paris on the model of an institutional research enterprise funded directly by the French crown. Roger Hahn's *The Anatomy of a Scientific Institution: The Paris Academy of Sciences 1666-1803* (California, 1971) dossiers the founding and the history. The Académie operated under direct royal patronage and direct royal funding for over a century, until the French Revolution dissolved it in 1793.

On 11 July 1700, Frederick I of Prussia founded the Königlich-Preußische Sozietät der Wissenschaften — the Berlin Academy — on the advice of Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz, who served as the first president. The Berlin Academy is the third founding vehicle of European empirical inquiry, alongside the Royal Society and the Académie des Sciences.

A fourth founding institution preceded all three. The Accademia del Cimento at Florence, founded in 1657 by Leopoldo de' Medici and operating with the surviving disciples of Galileo, ran for ten years until 1667 before being dissolved when Leopoldo was made a cardinal. W. E. Knowles Middleton's *The Experimenters* (Johns Hopkins, 1971) dossiers the Florentine institution. The Cimento ran the first systematic experimental research programme on the modern model; its dissolution after a decade is the evidence of how institutional inquiry has been at risk of capture or termination by political-religious authority from the founding moment.

The eighteenth and nineteenth centuries built out the architecture. The Russian Imperial Academy of Sciences was founded at St Petersburg in 1724 under Peter the Great. The Stockholm Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences was founded in 1739. The American Philosophical Society was founded at Philadelphia in 1743 by Benjamin Franklin. The Linnean Society of London was founded in 1788. The Royal Society of Edinburgh was founded in 1783. The Royal Astronomical Society was founded in 1820. The British Association for the Advancement of Science was founded in 1831; the American Association for the Advancement of Science in 1848. By 1900 every European nation and most of the Americas and parts of Asia had national scientific academies; the modern apparatus of inquiry was established.

The peer-reviewed journal system grew up alongside the academies. *Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society* was founded in 1665 by Henry Oldenburg as the Royal Society's publication of record. *Journal des Sçavans* was founded in Paris in the same year. The two are the founding scientific journals on the record. *Comptes Rendus de l'Académie des Sciences* was founded in 1835. *Nature* was founded on 4 November 1869 by Norman Lockyer. *Science* was founded on 3 July 1880 by an editorial circle around Thomas Edison and refounded in 1900 by James McKeen Cattell. Alex Csiszar's *The Scientific Journal: Authorship and the Politics of Knowledge in the Nineteenth Century* (Chicago, 2018) dossiers the history of the peer-reviewed journal system. Melinda Baldwin's *Making Nature* (Chicago, 2015) dossiers *Nature*'s editorial history.

What needs naming about the institutionalisation is the funding architecture from the start. The Royal Society operated under royal patronage but without significant royal funding; its early operations depended on member subscriptions and what its fellows could fund through their own resources. The Académie des Sciences operated under direct crown funding from 1666 onward — institutional inquiry as a state-funded enterprise. The Berlin Academy operated under Prussian crown funding from 1700. The Russian Imperial Academy operated under czarist funding from 1724. From the founding moment the institutionalisation of empirical inquiry has been tied to state and royal patronage; the early academic-industrial integration documented in Larry Stewart's *The Rise of Public Science* (Cambridge, 1992) shows the same dynamic in the British context, where Royal Society fellows' empirical work was sponsored by commercial interests from the late seventeenth century onward. Margaret Jacob's *Scientific Culture and the Making of the Industrial West* (Oxford, 1997) dossiers the broader integration across the eighteenth century. Mary Terrall's *The Man Who Flattened the Earth* (Chicago, 2002) dossiers the Maupertuis-led expeditions and the Académie's integration with French naval and colonial interests.

The early institutional inquiry produced findings the modern world rests on. Newton's *Principia* (1687), Lavoisier's chemical reorganisation in the 1780s, Galvani and Volta's electrical work in the 1790s, Dalton's atomic theory in 1808, Faraday's electromagnetic work from the 1820s through the 1850s. Maxwell's electromagnetic-equation synthesis in 1865, Darwin's *On the Origin of Species* in 1859, Mendeleev's periodic table in 1869, Pasteur's germ theory in the 1860s and 1870s, the Curies' radioactivity work from 1896, the early-twentieth-century reorganisation of physics by Planck, Einstein, Bohr, Schrödinger, Heisenberg, and the named others. Every named foundational finding of modern science was produced under institutional patronage, by working observers operating in the institutions the seventeenth-century founders built. The structure made possible observational work at scales the pre-institutional configurations could not reach. The apparatus has been a working condition for most of modern empirical inquiry.

The apparatus has also, from the founding moment, been entangled with state and capital. That is not the same thing as captured. The volume's argument is the more specific one. The institutional apparatus is the substrate. The co-opted apparatus is what has been built on top of the substrate across the period dossiered in Chapters 2 through 13. The captured apparatus operates by routing the social authority of empirical inquiry — which the substrate generates through genuine inquiry — into the four-century mechanism Volume One named. The co-opted apparatus is not the substrate. The substrate is the working observer producing the genuine finding. The captured apparatus is what has been built on top of the substrate to convert the finding's social authority into the operating posture the volume documents.

The reader who finishes this book is asked to hold the distinction. Institutional empirical inquiry has produced shared ground observation across the modern period. The volume honours the inquiry. The volume documents what has been built on top of the inquiry. The two are different.


## V. The line between empirical inquiry and consensus

The volume needs you to hold one more thing before the historical chapters begin.

Empirical inquiry and consensus can come apart.
