The history of the Scientific Revolution and the subsequent four centuries of the work contains documented cases in which the consensus at a given moment was wrong and a minority of working observers were right, and the empirical record across subsequent decades vindicated the minority. These are not cases of "anti-science." They are cases in which the working observer at the bench did the empirical work, the consensus held a contrary position for reasons, and the empirical record — confirmed across independent sites and independent investigators — eventually settled where the working observer had pointed. The cases are documentary anchors of the central distinction.

Four anchor cases.

On 29 May 1919 the British Royal Society sent an expedition to photograph a total solar eclipse at Príncipe (off the West African coast) and Sobral (in northeast Brazil) to test Albert Einstein's general-relativistic prediction that gravity would deflect starlight passing near the Sun. The expedition was led by Arthur Eddington at Príncipe and Andrew Crommelin at Sobral. The consensus in physics at the time held the Newtonian gravitational framework. Einstein, a German-Jewish physicist at the University of Berlin, had published the general theory of relativity in 1915 — during the middle of the First World War, in which Britain and Germany were enemy combatants. The eclipse measurements at Príncipe and Sobral confirmed Einstein's prediction; the Royal Society announcement on 6 November 1919 communicated the result. The documentation is F. W. Dyson, A. S. Eddington, and C. Davidson, "A Determination of the Deflection of Light by the Sun's Gravitational Field," *Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A,* 220(571-581), 1920, pp. 291-333. The case is a paradigm of shared ground observation across institutional and political divides. A British scientific institution, one year after the end of a war with Germany, confirmed a German Jewish physicist's prediction against the prior majority position because the empirical measurement at independent observation sites showed what it showed. The consensus shifted to accommodate the empirical finding. That is how the line operates when it operates correctly.

In 1925, Edwin Hubble at the Mount Wilson Observatory in California measured the distance to the Andromeda Nebula (M31) using Cepheid variable stars with the period-luminosity relation Henrietta Swan Leavitt had discovered at the Harvard College Observatory in 1908 and published in 1912. Hubble's measurement, published as "Cepheids in Spiral Nebulae" in *Publications of the American Astronomical Society* 5 (1925), pp. 261-264, established that Andromeda is around one million light-years from Earth — far outside the Milky Way — and that the universe is therefore vastly larger than the prior consensus had held. The prior consensus, dominant at the Harvard College Observatory under Harlow Shapley, was that the universe was confined to the Milky Way galaxy and that the spiral nebulae were objects within it. The 26 April 1920 Shapley-Curtis debate at the Smithsonian had aired the question; Shapley had been the more established figure. Hubble's measurement, building on Leavitt's pre-WWI discovery, settled the question. The consensus shifted. The case is another paradigm of the line operating correctly — and a paradigm of how the work of an institutionally-marginal woman computer (Leavitt was a Harvard "computer" paid for piecework calculations at low wages, not a faculty astronomer) is what made the subsequent finding possible.

In 1957, Chien-Shiung Wu at the National Bureau of Standards and Columbia University conducted an experiment confirming a theoretical prediction by Tsung-Dao Lee and Chen-Ning Yang that parity is not conserved in weak nuclear interactions. The prior consensus in physics had held parity conservation as a fundamental symmetry of nature; Wolfgang Pauli had remarked before the Wu experiment that he refused to believe parity violation could be real. Wu's experiment, published as "Experimental Test of Parity Conservation in Beta Decay" in *Physical Review* 105(4), 1957, pp. 1413-1415, confirmed Lee and Yang's prediction against the prior majority position. The consensus shifted. Lee and Yang received the Nobel Prize in Physics later in 1957; Wu, whose experimental confirmation made the recognition possible, was not included.

In 1980, Luis Alvarez, Walter Alvarez, Frank Asaro, and Helen V. Michel at Berkeley published "Extraterrestrial Cause for the Cretaceous-Tertiary Extinction" in *Science* 208(4448), 1980, pp. 1095-1108, proposing that an asteroid impact had caused the K-Pg mass extinction sixty-six million years ago, on the basis of the documented iridium anomaly at the K-Pg boundary at Gubbio, Italy. The proposal was rejected by the contemporary palaeontological consensus, which held that mass extinctions were intrinsically gradual processes driven by terrestrial causes. Across the subsequent decade independent geological work at sites across multiple countries — Stevns Klint in Denmark, Caravaca in Spain, multiple New Zealand sites, the Brazos River in Texas — independently confirmed the iridium anomaly at the K-Pg boundary. In 1991, Hildebrand, Penfield, and the named other workers identified the Chicxulub crater on the Yucatán Peninsula as the impact site. By 2010 the consensus had shifted: a Schulte et al. *Science* paper signed by forty-one named authors, "The Chicxulub Asteroid Impact and Mass Extinction at the Cretaceous-Paleogene Boundary," *Science* 327(5970), 2010, pp. 1214-1218, formalised the impact-extinction account as the consensus position. The case is the most named twentieth-century instance of a minority empirical finding, advanced against an entrenched consensus, being confirmed across independent observation sites and eventually accepted.

In each of these cases the institution that initially held a contrary consensus eventually accepted the empirical finding. The structure operated, eventually, as designed: shared ground observation at independent sites by independent observers settled the question, the consensus moved to accommodate the empirical record, and the working observer's finding was vindicated. These are cases of empirical inquiry operating despite consensus, not against it. The institutional structure is the substrate that made the empirical work possible. The consensus at a given moment is a social product that can be wrong. The volume names the distinction because the contemporary captured apparatus has, in named cases the later chapters dossier, presented consensus as if it were the empirical record itself, and has worked to suppress dissent that the empirical record would later vindicate.

There is a complementary set of cases the volume must also name, because the line cuts in both directions.

The 1989 cold-fusion announcement by Stanley Pons and Martin Fleischmann at the University of Utah claimed sustained nuclear fusion at room temperature in a tabletop electrolytic cell. The consensus did not initially have a position; the announcement bypassed normal peer review through a press conference on 23 March 1989. Independent replication attempts across the subsequent year, at named institutions including MIT, Caltech, Brookhaven National Laboratory, and dozens of named other sites, failed to reproduce the claimed effect under controlled conditions. The consensus settled, correctly, that the original finding had not been replicated. The case is the opposite of the four anchor cases above: a working-observer claim that did not survive the independent-replication test, and the consensus settling against it on the basis of the record of failed replications. The line operated correctly: the empirical record across independent sites settled the question.

The contemporary anti-vaccine movement based on Andrew Wakefield's 1998 *Lancet* paper "Ileal-lymphoid-nodular hyperplasia, non-specific colitis, and pervasive developmental disorder in children" by Wakefield, Murch, Anthony, Linnell, Casson, Malik, Berelowitz, Dhillon, Thomson, Harvey, Valentine, Davies, and Walker-Smith, published in *The Lancet* 351(9103), 1998, pp. 637-641, claimed a link between the MMR vaccine and autism in twelve children. Across the subsequent twelve years independent replication attempts across multiple national health systems — Denmark (Madsen et al. *New England Journal of Medicine* 347, 2002, pp. 1477-1482, sample of 537,303 children), the United States, the United Kingdom, Japan, and named others — failed to reproduce the claimed association at population scale. The journalist Brian Deer at the *Sunday Times* documented across 2004-2010 that Wakefield had undisclosed financial interests, that the original twelve cases had been selected from litigation referrals, and that key data had been manipulated. *The Lancet* retracted the paper on 2 February 2010; the General Medical Council struck Wakefield from the medical register on 24 May 2010. The case is the most named contemporary instance of the line cutting against an empirical claim that did not survive independent replication and that the documented evidence showed had been fabricated. The consensus settled against Wakefield because the shared ground observation across independent national-health-system studies did not support the claim, and because the evidence showed the original work had been corrupted. The line operated correctly.

The volume names both directions of the line because both directions matter. The contemporary captured apparatus has, in some named cases, presented consensus as the empirical record to suppress working observers whose findings would eventually be vindicated. The contemporary anti-empirical discourse has, in other named cases, presented isolated unreplicated findings as if they constituted evidence against an consensus that is actually tracking the shared ground observation. Both errors collapse the distinction the volume is asking anyone to hold. The line is shared ground observation — what independent observers at independent sites have confirmed. The line is not the consensus as such. The line is not the dissenter from the consensus as such. The line is the empirical record across independent confirmations.

The contemporary working observers who have been documenting captured apparatus from within their disciplines. John Ioannidis at Stanford, Marcia Angell formerly of *The New England Journal of Medicine,* Richard Horton at *The Lancet,* Daniel Sarewitz at *The New Atlantis,* Ben Goldacre at Oxford, Vinay Prasad at the University of California San Francisco — are read in this volume for documented sources their work establishes. Goldacre's *Bad Science* (Fourth Estate, 2008) and *Bad Pharma* (Fourth Estate, 2012) dossier the pharmaceutical-industry capture of medical research from inside the discipline. Prasad's *Malignant: How Bad Policy and Bad Evidence Harm People with Cancer* (Johns Hopkins, 2020) dossiers the cancer-research apparatus from inside the discipline. These working observers operate inside the institution and document what the compromised apparatus has been doing to the empirical record. They are the figures whose work the volume is on behalf of. They are the figures who hold the line between empirical inquiry and consensus from within.

With the line named, the volume can proceed to the historical and contemporary documentation in Chapters 2 through 13. The reader is asked to keep the line in mind. The empirical record across independent confirmations is honoured. The captured institutional apparatus is documented. The two are not the same.

---


# Chapter 1.2. Inquiry and the Disciplined Question Across Traditions


## I. What inquiry is

If observation is what an observer sees and records, inquiry is the disciplined posing of the question that organises what the observer looks for. The two are inseparable in practice. The Babylonian astronomer did not record everything that happened in the sky; the astronomer recorded what the inquiry-tradition had taught the astronomer to look for — risings, settings, conjunctions, eclipses, anomalies relative to the previously-recorded pattern. The inquiry organised the observation. Without the inquiry, the observation would have been undifferentiated. Without the observation, the inquiry would have been speculation.

The evidence covers what inquiry was before the consolidation Chapter 4 documents. Like observation, inquiry is older than the institutions that now claim to license it. Inquiry is older than peer review. Inquiry is older than the doctoral degree. Inquiry is older than the academic department. Inquiry is older than the credentialled professional class. Inquiry is what a human being does when they confront the world and want to know what is actually there.

The analysis operates on the same standard as Chapter 1.1. Each inquiry-tradition the chapter names is one that produced findings subsequent observers and inquirers across institutional and temporal divides confirmed, extended, or revised against further evidence. The analysis does not claim that every pre-institutional inquiry-tradition was correct. The record documents that the disciplined question, the willingness to be wrong, the willingness to revise on further evidence — the working procedures of inquiry — are older than the apparatus that now wraps them.


## II. Inquiry as method: the cross-cultural record

Across the traditions that produced sustained inquiry-records, several procedural features recur. Each was named, in distinct vocabularies, by the working observers within the tradition. The cross-cultural pattern is the historical evidence that inquiry operates as a working procedure independent of any particular institutional consolidation.

**The disciplined question.** An inquiry begins with a question that can be answered — by observation, by computation, by experiment, by argument from agreed premises. The question is constrained by what the inquirer can actually examine. Aristotle in the *Posterior Analytics* (Book I, chapters 1-3, c. 350 BCE, in W. D. Ross's Oxford edition 1949) named the constraint as the difference between *ti esti* (what is the thing?) and *dia ti* (why is it as it is?). The first question seeks definition; the second question seeks cause. The inquirer must know which question is being posed before any procedure can be applied to answer it. Ibn al-Haytham at Cairo in the *Kitāb al-Manāẓir* opened with the procedural distinction (in A. I. Sabra's Warburg Institute 1989 edition, Book I.1): the inquirer must "find the truth diligently" and "submit to argument and demonstration and not to the sayings of human beings" — a procedural commitment that cuts across the appeal-to-authority procedures of the surrounding intellectual milieu. The Confucian *Da Xue* (*Great Learning*) names the procedure *gewu zhizhi* — "the investigation of things and the extension of knowledge" — through the disciplined examination of named objects to extend understanding of the principles underlying them. The Vedic *Mīmāṃsā* tradition, articulated in Jaimini's *Mīmāṃsā Sūtras* (c. 200 BCE) and elaborated through Śabara, Kumārila, and Prabhākara across the seventh through eighth centuries CE, named six *pramāṇas* — modes of valid knowing — and the procedural criteria each could establish. The Buddhist *pramāṇa* school under Dignāga (c. 480-540 CE) in the *Pramāṇasamuccaya* and Dharmakīrti (c. 600-660 CE) in the *Pramāṇavārttika* refined the criteria further: perception (*pratyakṣa*) and inference (*anumāna*) as the two reliable means, with strict procedural criteria for the validity of each. The Chinese textual-critical tradition under Wang Chong (27-c. 100 CE) in the *Lunheng* (*Critical Essays*) explicitly named the inquiry-procedure: "What does one rely on in talking about anything? One must rely on what is true, one must investigate the facts." Wang Chong's *Lunheng* dossiered, in roughly eighty-five surviving chapters, the patterns by which the inquirer can be misled — by social authority, by traditional repetition, by emotional appeal — and named the procedural criteria for inquiry that does not get misled.

**The provisional answer.** An inquiry produces an answer; the answer is provisional, subject to revision on further evidence. The Buddhist Madhyamaka tradition under Nāgārjuna (c. 150-250 CE) in the *Mūlamadhyamakakārikā* named the procedural commitment in the strongest form: every claim is held provisionally; every position is examined for its internal coherence; the inquirer holds positions only as long as they bear examination. The Pyrrhonist tradition in the Greek-speaking Mediterranean, transmitted through Sextus Empiricus's *Outlines of Pyrrhonism* (c. 200 CE, in R. G. Bury's Loeb edition 1933), named the procedural commitment as *epoche* — suspension of judgement where the evidence does not establish the case. The Islamic *kalam* tradition under the Muʿtazilites and later under al-Ghazālī (1058-1111) in the *Tahāfut al-Falāsifa* (1095, in Michael E. Marmura's BYU Press 1997 translation) held the position that any inquiry-claim must be defended against the strongest available counter-argument; an undefended claim is not a claim. The Tibetan Buddhist *rang stong / gzhan stong* debates from the fourteenth century onward, dossiered in Karl Brunnhölzl's *The Center of the Sunlit Sky* (Snow Lion, 2004), formalised the disputation-procedure as the standard inquiry-method across centuries of working monastic-philosophical training. The Western scholastic *quaestiones disputatae* tradition, codified in the medieval universities of Paris, Oxford, Bologna, and Salamanca from the twelfth century onward, did the same: the inquirer states the position, names the strongest objections, defends the position against the objections, and revises the position where the objections hold. The inquiry is provisional. The inquiry continues as long as further evidence and further argument can be brought to bear.

**The check against the predecessor.** An inquiry checks itself against what previous inquirers have established. Hipparchus checked his observations against the Babylonian record. Ptolemy checked his against Hipparchus. The Maragha astronomers checked theirs against Ptolemy. Each inquirer treated the predecessor's record as evidence to be tested, not as authority to be deferred to. The Indian astronomical-mathematical tradition under Āryabhaṭa, Brahmagupta, Bhāskara II, and the Kerala school dossiered in Kim Plofker's *Mathematics in India* (Princeton, 2009) operated through the same procedure across the same fifteen centuries — each inquirer working through the predecessor's calculations, verifying where they held, correcting where they did not. The Talmudic tradition codified the check-against-the-predecessor procedure in the structure of the Babylonian Talmud itself: the Mishnah (codified c. 220 CE) records the inquirer-disputes of the Tannaim; the Gemara (codified c. 500 CE) records the inquirer-disputes of the Amoraim on the Mishnah; the medieval Tosafists (c. 1100-1300 CE) record the inquirer-disputes on both. Each layer is the check of the next generation of inquirers against the predecessor record. The procedure produced, across roughly fifteen centuries, the record of a working inquiry-tradition operating across the structures of multiple political environments.

**The willingness to be wrong.** The inquirer must hold the inquiry-claim loosely enough that further evidence can dislodge it. Charles Sanders Peirce in the late nineteenth-century United States named the procedural commitment as *fallibilism* — the working assumption that any particular inquiry-claim may turn out to be wrong, that the inquiry-procedure must therefore be designed to permit correction, that the inquirer who refuses correction has stopped doing inquiry. Peirce's account, articulated in "The Fixation of Belief" (*Popular Science Monthly*, November 1877) and "How to Make Our Ideas Clear" (*Popular Science Monthly*, January 1878), names a procedural commitment that the cross-cultural record dossiers across the prior two thousand years. The Buddhist *pramāṇa* tradition held it. The Confucian *gewu* tradition held it. The Islamic *kalam* tradition held it. The Talmudic *machloket* (disputation) tradition held it. The Greek *epoche* tradition held it. The Indian *Mīmāṃsā* and *Nyāya* traditions held it. Inquiry that does not permit correction by further evidence is not inquiry. It is something else.

These four procedural features — the disciplined question, the provisional answer, the check against the predecessor, the willingness to be wrong — recur across the inquiry-traditions that produced sustained findings subsequent inquirers confirmed and extended. They are not the property of any one tradition. They are not a Western invention. They are a description of what working inquiry has actually looked like across the cultures and centuries that produced working inquiry-records.


## III. The Greek tradition

The Greek inquiry-tradition is dossiered at greater depth in the cross-cultural record than the other pre-institutional traditions because the European institutional consolidation that Chapter 4 documents grew out of the Greek tradition via Islamic transmission. The depth in the European-language documentary record is the consequence of subsequent European institutional self-narration. The depth is real; the implied uniqueness is not. The Greek tradition is one tradition among the several the cross-cultural record dossiers; the European institutional consolidation is the consequence of subsequent capture, not of the inquiry-tradition itself.

G. E. R. Lloyd's two-volume *Early Greek Science: Thales to Aristotle* (Chatto & Windus, 1970) and *Greek Science After Aristotle* (Chatto & Windus, 1973) dossier the inquiry-tradition. The named figures: Thales of Miletus (c. 624-c. 546 BCE), Anaximander (c. 610-c. 546 BCE), Anaximenes (c. 586-c. 526 BCE), Pythagoras (c. 570-c. 495 BCE). Heraclitus (c. 535-c. 475 BCE), Parmenides (c. 515-c. 450 BCE), Empedocles (c. 494-c. 434 BCE), Anaxagoras (c. 500-c. 428 BCE), Democritus (c. 460-c. 370 BCE). Hippocrates of Cos (c. 460-c. 370 BCE), Socrates (c. 470-399 BCE), Plato (428/427-348/347 BCE), Aristotle (384-322 BCE), Epicurus (341-270 BCE), Pyrrho (c. 360-c. 270 BCE). Eratosthenes (c. 276-c. 194 BCE), Archimedes (c. 287-c. 212 BCE), Hipparchus (c. 190-c. 120 BCE), Galen (129-c. 216 CE), Ptolemy (c. 100-c. 170 CE), Sextus Empiricus (c. 160-c. 210 CE).

The procedural innovations the Greek tradition contributed to the cross-cultural inquiry-record: the demonstrative-mathematical proof (Euclid's *Elements* c. 300 BCE, dossiered in Reviel Netz's *The Shaping of Deduction in Greek Mathematics,* Cambridge, 1999); the controlled clinical observation tradition (the Hippocratic Corpus, with the *Epidemics* I and III containing forty-two named patient case histories. The earliest extant clinical case-record tradition); the systematic biological classification (Aristotle's *Historia Animalium,* *De Partibus Animalium,* *De Generatione Animalium,* dossiered in Allan Gotthelf and James G. Lennox's *Philosophical Issues in Aristotle's Biology,* Cambridge, 1987); the geometric astronomy that produced computable models (Eudoxus's homocentric spheres, Hipparchus's epicycle-and-deferent system, Ptolemy's *Almagest*); the explicit articulation of the inquiry-method itself (Aristotle's *Posterior Analytics*).

The Greek tradition did not invent inquiry. The Greek tradition produced a particular documentary articulation of what inquiry is and a particular set of procedural innovations that subsequent inquirers across cultures inherited. The Babylonian astronomical tradition that the Greek tradition inherited was already a working inquiry-tradition. The Egyptian surveying and engineering tradition that the Greek tradition inherited was already a working inquiry-tradition. The Phoenician maritime tradition that the Greek tradition inherited was already a working inquiry-tradition. What the Greek tradition contributed was a distinctive procedural and documentary articulation of inquiry-method that the subsequent transmission preserved. The Greek tradition did not invent the procedure. The Greek tradition documented the procedure in a form that subsequent inquirers across cultures could read.


## IV. The Indian tradition

The Indian inquiry-tradition operated as a continuous record across roughly two and a half millennia, from the *Upaniṣads* of the late Vedic period (c. 800-300 BCE) through the navya-nyāya logical tradition that the British colonial encounter interrupted in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. The depth of the record is comparable to the Greek tradition; the survival is comparable; the procedural innovations are comparable. The under-coverage of the Indian inquiry-tradition in standard European-language histories of the inquiry-method is the consequence of subsequent institutional capture, not of any difference in the record itself.

The six *darśanas* — the six orthodox philosophical schools of classical Indian inquiry — dossiered the working inquiry-procedure across centuries. Bimal Krishna Matilal's *The Character of Logic in India* (SUNY Press, 1998), Karl H. Potter's eighteen-volume *Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies* (Motilal Banarsidass, 1970-onward), Jonardon Ganeri's *The Lost Age of Reason* (Oxford, 2011) dossier the record. The six schools — *Nyāya* (logic and epistemology), *Vaiśeṣika* (atomistic natural philosophy), *Sāṃkhya* (analysis of categories), *Yoga* (the procedural-meditative tradition), *Pūrva Mīmāṃsā* (textual and procedural analysis), *Uttara Mīmāṃsā* or *Vedānta* (the analysis of ultimate referent) — each named procedures for inquiry, criteria for valid knowing, criteria for invalid argumentation, criteria for the resolution of disputation.

The Nyāya tradition under Gautama Akṣapāda (the *Nyāya Sūtras,* c. 200 BCE - 200 CE) named the five-membered syllogism (*pañcāvayava*) — proposition, reason, example, application, conclusion — that the working logician used to demonstrate inference. The procedural step that the Indian tradition contributed beyond the Aristotelian syllogism is the explicit empirical-example step: the inference is valid only when the example (*udāharaṇa*) can be exhibited as a case where the relation between reason and conclusion has been observed. The procedural commitment to empirical illustration is documented across the navya-nyāya elaboration under Gangeśa Upādhyāya (c. 1325 CE) in the *Tattvacintāmaṇi* and the subsequent navya-nyāya commentarial tradition through the eighteenth century.

The Buddhist *pramāṇa* tradition under Dignāga (c. 480-540 CE) in the *Pramāṇasamuccaya* and Dharmakīrti (c. 600-660 CE) in the *Pramāṇavārttika* contributed the rigorous procedural definition of valid perception (*pratyakṣa*) and valid inference (*anumāna*), with criteria for invalid perception (the senses can be deceived, the inference can rest on insufficient evidence) and criteria for the resolution of disputation. The tradition is dossiered in Tom J. F. Tillemans's *Scripture, Logic, Language* (Wisdom Publications, 1999) and in Georges B. J. Dreyfus's *Recognizing Reality* (SUNY Press, 1997).

The procedural commitment to disputation as the working method — public debate between named positions, with named criteria for who has prevailed — was institutionalised in the Buddhist monastic universities of Nālandā (c. 5th-12th centuries CE), Vikramaśilā (c. 8th-12th centuries CE), and Odantapura (c. 8th-12th centuries CE), and continued in the Tibetan monastic university tradition through the contemporary period. Daniel Perdue's *Debate in Tibetan Buddhism* (Snow Lion, 1992) dossiers the contemporary working procedure. The procedural rigor at the Tibetan monastic universities under the Gelug, Sakya, Kagyu, and Nyingma traditions is, at the working level, the documentary continuation of an inquiry-method articulated in India fifteen centuries ago, preserved through institutional transmission across the Himalayan plateau, and operating today as a working inquiry-procedure.


## V. The Chinese tradition

The Chinese inquiry-tradition operated as a continuous record across roughly two and a half millennia, from the *Mohist Canons* of the late Warring States period (c. 300 BCE) through the *kaozheng* (evidential-research) movement of the Qing dynasty in the seventeenth through nineteenth centuries. The depth of the record is comparable to the Greek and Indian traditions. The procedural innovations are distinctive. The under-coverage in standard European-language histories of inquiry-method is, again, the consequence of subsequent institutional capture, not of the record itself.

The *Mohist Canons* (*Mojing*), composed by the later Mohists in the period c. 350-200 BCE and dossiered in A. C. Graham's *Later Mohist Logic, Ethics and Science* (Chinese University Press, 1978), contributed the earliest extant Chinese articulation of inquiry-procedure. The Canons named criteria for definition, for inference, for the resolution of disputation, for the conduct of experimental investigation. The work was largely lost during the Han-dynasty consolidation under Confucian state orthodoxy and recovered as an inquiry-tradition only in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.

The Confucian textual-critical tradition under Wang Chong (27-c. 100 CE) in the *Lunheng,* Liu Zhiji (661-721 CE) in the *Shitong* (*Generalities of Historical Method*), and the later Song-dynasty under Zhu Xi (1130-1200) developed the documentary-critical inquiry-procedures. The procedural commitment to *gewu zhizhi* — the investigation of things and the extension of knowledge — articulated in the *Da Xue* and elaborated by Zhu Xi in the *Sishu Jizhu* and by Wang Yangming (1472-1529) in the *Chuanxilu* names the inquiry-procedure as the disciplined examination of named objects (the *wu* — things, phenomena, particulars) to extend the inquirer's understanding of the underlying *li* (pattern, principle).

The Qing-dynasty *kaozheng* movement under Gu Yanwu (1613-1682), Yan Ruoju (1636-1704), Hui Dong (1697-1758), Dai Zhen (1724-1777), and Duan Yucai (1735-1815) developed a documentary-critical inquiry-procedure that, dossiered in Benjamin Elman's *From Philosophy to Philology* (Council on East Asian Studies, Harvard, 1984. Revised UCLA 2001), paralleled the contemporaneous European philological tradition in its procedural rigor. The *kaozheng* scholars subjected the classical Chinese textual canon to systematic philological examination — etymological, phonological, archaeological, intertextual — to determine which texts were authentic, which were later forgeries, which had been corrupted in transmission. Yan Ruoju's *Shangshu Guwen Shuzheng* (1745) demonstrated, on philological evidence, that sixteen of the chapters of the classical *Shangshu* (*Book of Documents*) ascribed to ancient sources were later forgeries — a finding the Qing-dynasty Chinese institutional consensus eventually accepted on the evidence the *kaozheng* tradition produced.

The Chinese tradition of natural-philosophical inquiry, dossiered in Joseph Needham's twenty-seven-volume *Science and Civilisation in China* (Cambridge, 1954-2015), produced documented findings in mathematics (the *Jiu Zhang Suan Shu*. *Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art,* compiled c. 200 BCE-200 CE), astronomy (the continuous court-archive astronomical record across two millennia), medicine (the *Huangdi Neijing*. *Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon,* compiled c. 200 BCE-200 CE), pharmacology (Li Shizhen's *Bencao Gangmu* — *Compendium of Materia Medica,* 1578, with 1,892 named substances dossiered), seismology (Zhang Heng's seismoscope of 132 CE), magnetism (the first documented magnetic-compass for navigation in the eleventh-century *Wujing Zongyao*), and named other domains. The findings were produced by working inquirers operating in lineages — court astronomical bureaus, physician families, scholarly networks — outside institutional structures of the modern type.


## VI. The Islamic tradition

The Islamic inquiry-tradition from c. 750 CE to c. 1500 CE — the Islamic Golden Age — operated across the Abbasid caliphate at Baghdad, the Umayyad caliphate at Córdoba, the Fatimid caliphate at Cairo, the Seljuk and Ilkhanate Persian dynasties, the early Ottoman period, and the Mughal Indian period. The procedural innovations were considerable. The transmission to the subsequent European institutional consolidation was direct. The under-coverage in standard European-language histories of inquiry-method is the consequence of subsequent capture, not of the record.

Dimitri Gutas's *Greek Thought, Arabic Culture* (Routledge, 1998) and George Saliba's *Islamic Science and the Making of the European Renaissance* (MIT Press, 2007) dossier the tradition. The named figures: al-Khwārizmī (c. 780-c. 850), al-Kindī (c. 801-c. 873), al-Rāzī (c. 854-c. 925), al-Fārābī (c. 872-c. 950), Ibn Sīnā / Avicenna (c. 980-1037). Al-Bīrūnī (973-c. 1050), Ibn al-Haytham / Alhazen (c. 965-c. 1040), al-Ghazālī (1058-1111), Ibn Rushd / Averroës (1126-1198), Naṣīr al-Dīn al-Ṭūsī (1201-1274), Ibn al-Shāṭir (1304-1375), Ulugh Beg (1394-1449), Taqī al-Dīn (1526-1585).

Ibn al-Haytham's *Kitāb al-Manāẓir* (*Book of Optics,* c. 1011-1021) articulated the procedural commitment to controlled experimental investigation in optics: the inquirer constructs the experimental apparatus, observes the phenomenon, varies the parameters, records the result, and reports the procedure in such detail that another inquirer can reproduce it. The procedural innovation is the explicit requirement of reproducibility by a different inquirer. Ibn al-Haytham's procedure is dossiered, with the experimental apparatus reconstructed, in A. I. Sabra's two-volume Warburg Institute edition (1989) and in Mark Smith's translation in the *Transactions of the American Philosophical Society* series (2001-onward).

Al-Bīrūnī, working at Ghazni and Multan under the Ghaznavid dynasty in the early eleventh century, produced documented inquiry in astronomy, geography, geodesy, mineralogy, and Indology. His *Kitāb Taḥdīd Nihāyāt al-Amākin* (*Book of the Determination of Coordinates of Cities*) dossiers his procedural method for determining geographic coordinates from astronomical observations. His *Kitāb fī Taḥqīq mā li'l-Hind* (*Book of Inquiry into India,* c. 1030, in Edward C. Sachau's two-volume translation 1888 reprinted by Kegan Paul) is the earliest extensive cross-cultural comparative work in the European-language documentary record — al-Bīrūnī learned Sanskrit, studied with named Indian pundits, and produced the dossier of Indian astronomy, mathematics, philosophy, and religion that the European-language world would later inherit through Latin translation.

The Maragha observatory under Naṣīr al-Dīn al-Ṭūsī from 1259 and the Damascus tradition under Ibn al-Shāṭir from c. 1350 developed the planetary models that Copernicus would inherit. F. Jamil Ragep's "Copernicus and his Islamic Predecessors" (*History of Science,* 45(1), 2007) and Saliba's *Islamic Science and the Making of the European Renaissance* dossier the transmission. The Ṭūsī couple — the geometric device for representing linear motion as the combination of two circular motions — appears in Copernicus's *De Revolutionibus* of 1543 in exactly the form Ibn al-Shāṭir articulated in the fourteenth century. The transmission across cultures, across centuries, across institutional structures — the working inquiry-procedure operating independent of any one institutional consolidation — is documented across the surviving manuscript record.


## VII. The Talmudic tradition

The Talmudic inquiry-tradition operated as a continuous record across roughly two and a half millennia, from the Pharisaic and proto-rabbinic period (c. 200 BCE) through the contemporary yeshiva tradition. The procedural innovations are distinctive — the most fully developed disputation-procedure on the cross-cultural record. The procedural commitment to disagreement as the working method is documented in the structure of the Talmud itself.

The Mishnah, codified c. 220 CE under Judah ha-Nasi at Bet Sheʿarim and Tzippori in the Galilee, dossiers the inquirer-disputes of the Tannaim — the rabbinic figures of the first two centuries CE. The Gemara, codified c. 500 CE in the two recensions of the Palestinian Talmud (Yerushalmi) and the Babylonian Talmud (Bavli), dossiers the inquirer-disputes of the Amoraim — the rabbinic figures of the third through fifth centuries CE — on the Mishnah. The medieval Tosafists at Troyes, Champagne, and broader northern France from c. 1100 to 1300 CE dossier the inquirer-disputes on both. The contemporary yeshiva tradition continues the same procedural method.

The Talmudic procedure has three procedural features the cross-cultural inquiry-record has not produced in the same systematic combination elsewhere:

**The named position with the named opponent.** The Mishnah and Gemara name each inquirer's position with the name of the inquirer (Rabbi Akiva, Rabbi Yishmael, Rabbi Meir, Rabbi Yehuda, Rabbi Yose, Rabbi Shimon, Rabbi Eliezer, Rabbi Yehoshua, Bet Hillel, Bet Shammai) and the opposing position with the name of the opponent. The procedural commitment is to the named record of who held what. The dispute is not anonymised, abstracted, or summarised. The dispute is dossiered in the working voices of the inquirers.

**The preservation of the rejected position.** The Talmud preserves the inquirer-positions that the subsequent halakhic consensus did not accept alongside the positions that were accepted. Bet Shammai's positions, almost uniformly rejected in the subsequent halakhic consensus, are preserved in the Talmudic record. The procedural commitment is that the rejected position is preserved because future inquirers may need to return to it — because the rejection was provisional, because further evidence may reopen the dispute, because the working procedure of the tradition is that the dispute continues across centuries.

**The procedural rule for resolution.** The Talmud names procedural rules for the resolution of disputes — by majority among the named contemporaries; by precedence among the named generations; by the appeal to evidence (the *raʾayah*) from the textual record; by the *kal vaḥomer* (a fortiori) inference; by the *gezerah shavah* (verbal analogy) inference; by the *binyan av* (paradigm-case) inference. The procedural rules are themselves subject to dispute and elaboration across the centuries of the tradition.

David Weiss Halivni's three-volume *Midrash, Mishnah, and Gemara* (Harvard, 1986), *Sources and Traditions* (multi-volume, JTS 1968-onward), and *Peshat and Derash* (Oxford, 1991) dossier the procedural method at the working level. The yeshiva tradition continues the procedural method in the contemporary period.

The relevant point for the present chapter is that the Talmudic tradition demonstrates inquiry as a sustained, named, documented procedure across two and a half millennia, operating independently of any institutional consolidation of the type Chapter 4 documents. The Talmudic tradition does not need peer-review to validate its procedure. The procedure is older than peer-review and operates by a different mechanism.


## VIII. The cross-cultural pattern

The record has dossiered five major inquiry-traditions — Greek, Indian, Chinese, Islamic, Talmudic — and named procedural features that recur across them. The record has dossiered the procedural commitment to the disciplined question, the provisional answer, the check against the predecessor, the willingness to be wrong. The record has dossiered the evidence at sufficient depth that you can verify what is claimed against named sources.

The pattern across the dossier is the pattern. Inquiry as a working procedure is older than the apparatus that now licenses it. Inquiry is not the property of any one culture, any one tradition, any one credentialled professional class. Inquiry is what human beings have been doing across cultures, across centuries, across institutional structures whenever they have been free to ask the disciplined question and to follow the evidence.

The co-opted apparatus this volume documents has, in the contemporary period, taken the inquiry-procedure that the pre-institutional cross-cultural record dossiered and wrapped it in the architecture Chapter 4 names. The wrapping has, in cases the volume dossiers across the per-sector chapters, transformed the inquiry-procedure into the consensus-engineering procedure. Has substituted the credentialled-professional vote for the disciplined question, has substituted the funded position for the provisional answer, has substituted the journal for the check against the predecessor, has substituted the career penalty for the willingness to be wrong. The inquiry-procedure operates underneath the co-opted apparatus where the seized apparatus has not penetrated. The procedure is older than the apparatus. The procedure survives the apparatus where individual working inquirers continue to ask the disciplined question, to hold the provisional answer, to check against the predecessor record, and to be willing to be wrong.

The reader who reads further in the volume is asked to keep this in mind. The captured apparatus that the volume dossiers is not what inquiry is. The co-opted apparatus is what was built on top of inquiry — within the four-century mechanism Volume One named, by the named operators the volume documents across the contemporary period. Underneath the capture, the procedure remains.

---


# Chapter 1.3. Recall, Oral Tradition, and the Transmission of Knowledge Across Generations


## I. What recall is

The two preceding chapters dossiered observation and inquiry. Both presuppose what this chapter takes as its subject: the capacity of a human community to hold knowledge across generations, to pass the working procedures from the inquirer who developed them to the inquirer who will inherit and extend them, to maintain the documented evidence across the centuries during which institutional structures rise and fall and rise again. That capacity is recall — the act of remembering and the procedural-cultural architecture by which a community remembers.

Recall in the sense the chapter uses is not the cognitive capacity of an individual brain to retrieve stored information. Recall in the sense the chapter uses is the working procedure by which a community maintains its knowledge against the entropy of time, individual mortality, political disruption, and institutional capture. The community remembers because it has procedures for remembering. The procedures are older than the institutions that now claim to license memory. They survive the institutions where the institutions fail.

What follows covers the cross-cultural record of the procedural architecture by which communities have held knowledge across generations. The analysis dossiers the oral-traditional cultures whose recall-procedures preserved findings across millennia without writing. The chapter dossiers the written-traditional cultures whose recall-procedures combined memorisation and the written archive. The chapter dossiers the contemporary period during which the displacement of recall by externalised, captured, and algorithmically-mediated information-retrieval has begun to weaken the procedural-cultural architecture that the cross-cultural record dossiers.


## II. The oral-traditional cultures: a working procedure

The standard institutional histories of inquiry treat the oral-traditional cultures as the pre-historical background against which the writing-cultures developed the working procedures of inquiry. The treatment is wrong. The oral-traditional cultures developed procedures for holding knowledge across generations that, in the named cases the cross-cultural record dossiers, were as rigorous, as reliable, and as durable as the written-traditional procedures. The procedures were different. The procedures were not inferior.

**The Vedic oral tradition.** The earliest extant *Ṛgveda* is a collection of 1,028 hymns composed in the period c. 1500-1000 BCE in archaic Sanskrit. The work was transmitted entirely orally for roughly two thousand years before being committed to writing in the medieval period. The procedural method that preserved the text across two thousand years of oral transmission with almost zero textual variation across the geographically-distributed reciters is dossiered in Frits Staal's *Discovering the Vedas* (Penguin Books India, 2008) and in the prolonged documentary work of Michael Witzel at Harvard. The procedural method has several named components: the *jaṭā-pāṭha,* the *ghana-pāṭha,* and the *krama-pāṭha* — three distinct mnemonic permutation-patterns through which the reciter learns to recite the text not only in its standard sequence but in additional ordered patterns that would detect any textual corruption in the standard sequence. The procedural rigor of the *Vedic* oral-transmission method produced, across roughly two and a half millennia, a textual stability that the contemporary critical-philological examination of the surviving manuscript record has confirmed: the *Ṛgveda* as recited by named brāhman reciters in the contemporary period is, at the consonant level, the same text that the Vedic oral tradition transmitted in the archaic period.

**The Homeric oral tradition.** Milman Parry's documentary fieldwork in the 1930s among the South Slavic *guslari* — the working oral epic singers of Bosnia, Serbia, and Croatia — dossiered in Parry's *The Making of Homeric Verse* (Oxford, 1971, posthumously edited by his son Adam Parry) demonstrated the procedural architecture of oral epic composition. The *guslar* did not memorise a fixed text and recite it; the *guslar* held a working repertoire of formulaic phrases, narrative themes, and metrical patterns that the singer assembled in performance to produce the working epic. The procedural method permitted the oral tradition to maintain the epic across centuries through generations of singers without any one singer ever holding the entire text in memory in the modern textual sense. Albert B. Lord's *The Singer of Tales* (Harvard, 1960; second edition 2000) extended the record. The procedural method is the working procedure by which the *Iliad* and *Odyssey* were composed and transmitted across the centuries between the Mycenaean Bronze Age (the historical-narrative substrate, c. 1200 BCE) and the period in which the texts were written down (the seventh and sixth centuries BCE). The Greek institutional self-narration that places the Homeric epics at the founding of the Greek inquiry-tradition is correct on the chronology; it understates the procedural sophistication of the oral tradition that preserved the epics across roughly six centuries.

**The Polynesian oral-navigational tradition.** Chapter 1.1 dossiered the 1976 voyage of the Hokule'a as empirical confirmation of a Polynesian non-instrument navigation tradition that the European institutional consensus had treated as folkloric. The tradition is also a recall-tradition. The working knowledge that the Polynesian navigators held — the star compass with its thirty-two named points around the horizon, the swell patterns of the open Pacific, the seasonal bird-migration routes, the cloud patterns that signal islands beyond the horizon, the genealogy of voyage-courses across the Pacific — was held entirely in oral transmission across roughly three thousand years of Polynesian settlement-history from c. 1500 BCE to the colonial encounter with European institutional structures in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries CE. David Lewis's *We, the Navigators* (Hawaii, 1994 second edition) dossiers the working procedure. Mau Piailug of Satawal (1932-2010) was the last named master navigator who held the working tradition unbroken from the pre-contact period; his transmission of the procedure to the Polynesian Voyaging Society in Hawaii from the 1970s onward dossiers the procedural method at the working level.

**The Indigenous-Australian oral tradition.** Patrick Nunn and Nicholas Reid in "Aboriginal memories of inundation of the Australian coast dating from more than 7000 years ago" (*Australian Geographer,* 47(1), 2016) dossier the evidentiary case that named oral traditions across multiple Australian Aboriginal language groups preserve recognisable accounts of the post-glacial sea-level rise that flooded the continental shelves of the Australian coast in the period c. 11,000-7,000 BCE. The named traditions across the Yidiñ, Yirrganydji, Gunggandji, Bandjin, Warrgamay, Nyawaygi, Mara, Manjiljara, Ngarrindjeri, and Narrungga language groups preserve geographic detail — the position of named coastlines that the inundation displaced, the identification of named offshore islands that were once part of the mainland, the named drowned landmarks — at sufficient detail that the cross-tradition documentary record correlates with the contemporary geological reconstruction of the post-glacial inundation. The oral tradition has preserved documented sources across roughly three hundred to four hundred generations.

**The West African griot tradition.** The named Mande griots — Sundiata Keita's named genealogy-bearer tradition from the Mali Empire of the thirteenth century onward — preserved working genealogical, historical, and legal recall across centuries through professional-lineage transmission. The documentary record is dossiered in Djibril Tamsir Niane's *Sundiata: An Epic of Old Mali* (Longman, 1965, from the recitation of the named griot Mamadou Kouyaté) and in the sustained documentary work of D. T. Niane and the wider African-historiography tradition. The griot transmission method involves the apprentice-master transmission within named lineages — the Kouyaté, Diabaté, Cissoko, and named other griot lineages — across roughly twenty-five generations from the foundation of the Mali Empire to the contemporary period.

The pattern across the cross-cultural record is consistent. The oral-traditional cultures developed procedural methods for holding knowledge across generations. The methods worked. The depth of the evidence they preserved is, in cases the chapter has named, deeper than the depth the corresponding writing-culture institutional record produced.


## III. The written-traditional cultures: recall and the archive

The transition from oral-traditional to written-traditional recall did not eliminate the oral procedural architecture. The written-traditional cultures maintained both procedures in working combination across the periods their inquiry-records flourished. The Babylonian astronomical record on the cuneiform tablets was maintained by named scribal lineages who held the tablets in working memory in the act of consulting them — the tablets did not interpret themselves; the scribal community held the procedural-interpretive tradition by oral apprentice-master transmission alongside the written record. The Confucian classical-textual tradition in China was maintained by named lineages of textual scholars who memorised the classics in childhood and held the textual archive in working oral recall across their lives, alongside the written copies in the imperial libraries and the private libraries of named scholarly families. The Talmudic tradition dossiered in Chapter 1.2 held the written text of the Mishnah and Gemara alongside the oral *masorah* — the transmitted apparatus of pronunciation, cantillation, and interpretive tradition that the written text by itself does not encode.

The written archive does not replace the oral recall. The written archive is what the oral recall has produced as its working medium of cross-generational stability when the procedural rigor of the oral-traditional method becomes too demanding to maintain across the working population of inquirers a sustained tradition requires. The written archive permits the inquiry-tradition to extend beyond the bounded community of working oral inquirers. The written archive permits the inquiry-tradition to be checked by inquirers across cultures, across centuries, across institutional structures. The cross-cultural transmission Chapter 1.1 dossiered — Hipparchus checking Babylonian tablets, Ptolemy checking Hipparchus, Maragha checking Ptolemy, Copernicus checking Maragha — operated through the written archive as the working medium.

The written archive also has working limitations the oral-traditional procedure does not have. The written archive can be destroyed in ways the dispersed oral-traditional procedure cannot. The Library of Alexandria — the largest single archive of the classical Hellenistic inquiry-tradition — was lost across the period from the Roman civil wars of the first century BCE through the Arab conquest in the seventh century CE; the evidentiary detail of what was lost is dossiered in Roy MacLeod's *The Library of Alexandria* (I. B. Tauris, 2004) and Mostafa El-Abbadi's *Life and Fate of the Ancient Library of Alexandria* (UNESCO, 1990). The House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Ḥikma) at Baghdad — the principal Abbasid archive of the Islamic Golden Age inquiry-tradition — was destroyed in the Mongol sack of Baghdad in 1258; the evidentiary detail of the loss is dossiered in Hugh Kennedy's *When Baghdad Ruled the Muslim World* (Da Capo, 2005). The Nālandā monastic university library — the principal Buddhist archive of the Indian inquiry-tradition — was destroyed in the period c. 1193 CE by the Ghurid invasion under Bakhtiyar Khalji; the surviving sources for the destruction are dossiered in Hartmut Walravens's *Nalanda and Other Ancient Indian Buddhist Universities* (Harrassowitz, 2010). The Mexica codex archives — the principal repository of the pre-Columbian Mesoamerican inquiry-tradition — were burned by the Spanish conquistador and ecclesiastical administrators in the period c. 1520-1560; only fragmentary survivals remain. In each case, the loss of the archive destroyed the working continuity of an inquiry-tradition that the oral-traditional procedure could have preserved more durably if the procedure had been maintained at the working population level alongside the archive.

The procedural lesson from the cross-cultural record is that the working community of an inquiry-tradition needs both the oral-traditional procedural rigor and the written archive in combination. Either by itself is fragile in ways the combination is not. The reduction of recall to the written archive alone — and beyond that to the externalised, captured, algorithmically-mediated information-retrieval that the contemporary period has produced — is the working dissolution of the procedural architecture that the cross-cultural record dossiers as the procedural condition for sustained inquiry across generations.


## IV. The procedural commitments of recall

Across the cross-cultural record the chapter has dossiered, several procedural commitments recur in the recall-traditions that produced sustained working knowledge across generations.

**The named transmitter.** The recall-tradition operates through named transmitters whose authority is the record of their procedural rigor. The Vedic *brāhman* reciter is named within the lineage. The Talmudic *Mesoret* preserves the named transmitter at each generation of the *masorah*. The Confucian classical lineage names the master and the named student-transmitters. The Buddhist monastic lineage names the named teacher-student transmission at each generation across centuries. The Polynesian navigator names the named master from whom the working procedure was learned. The named transmitter is the working procedural assurance that the recall-tradition has not been corrupted between generations. The procedural commitment to naming the transmitter is the working procedural-accountability mechanism within the recall-tradition.

**The procedural redundancy.** The recall-tradition operates with procedural redundancy — multiple parallel procedures that can detect corruption in any one of them. The Vedic *jaṭā-pāṭha,* *ghana-pāṭha,* and *krama-pāṭha* permutation-patterns dossier the procedural redundancy at the working level: any corruption in the standard sequence is detected by the permutation-patterns; any corruption in any one permutation is detected by the others. The Talmudic preservation of the named opposing positions provides procedural redundancy: any subsequent corruption of one position is detected by comparison with the preserved opposing position. The cross-cultural inquiry-tradition that Chapter 1.1 dossiered operates with the same procedural redundancy at the inter-cultural level — the named observer at one site checks the predecessor record at another site, and the procedural redundancy across cultures detects corruption that any one cultural lineage might introduce.

**The procedural transparency.** The recall-tradition operates with procedural transparency — the working community can examine the procedure by which the recall operates. The Talmudic dialectical structure is procedurally transparent: you can see the working dispute, the named positions, the procedural rules for resolution, the textual evidence the disputants brought to bear. The Vedic recitation procedure is procedurally transparent: the reciter performs the permutation-patterns in such a way that a competent listener can verify the procedure is being followed. The Polynesian navigation procedure is procedurally transparent at the apprentice-master level: the apprentice navigator learns the procedure by working alongside the master across multi-year apprenticeships, and the procedure can be verified at each stage. The procedural transparency is the working procedural-accountability mechanism within the recall-tradition.

These three procedural commitments — the named transmitter, the procedural redundancy, the procedural transparency — recur across the cross-cultural record of recall-traditions. They are not the property of any one tradition. They are a description of what working recall has actually looked like across the cultures and centuries that produced working recall-records sustained across generations.


## V. The contemporary displacement of recall

The contemporary period dossiered across the subsequent chapters of this volume has produced a working displacement of the procedural architecture of recall by externalised, captured, and algorithmically-mediated information-retrieval. The displacement operates at the working level across multiple sites the volume dossiers in the subsequent chapters.

The replacement of the textbook-and-memory educational architecture with the search-engine-mediated information-retrieval architecture has, across the period from roughly 2000 to the contemporary period, produced a working population of inquirers for whom the procedural commitment to held knowledge. The working memory of the predecessor record, the procedural rigor of the recall-tradition — has been progressively displaced by the assumption that the relevant information is available on demand through external retrieval. The procedural condition that the cross-cultural record dossiers as the condition for sustained inquiry across generations. The working community holding the predecessor record in working memory while consulting the written archive — has been increasingly displaced by a working procedure in which the inquirer queries an externalised, captured, and algorithmically-mediated retrieval system and accepts the system's output as the working substrate of subsequent inquiry. Chapter 12 of this volume dossiers captured-search-and-algorithmic-curation architecture at the working level. The point for the present chapter is that the co-opted-search architecture operates as a working replacement for the procedural architecture of recall that the cross-cultural record dossiers as the procedural condition for sustained inquiry across generations.

The replacement of working memory by externalised retrieval is not, by itself, capture. The procedural displacement that capture introduces is the compromised-mediator's position between the inquirer and the externalised record. The algorithmic curation that ranks the retrieved results, the press source-prioritisation that determines what the externalised retrieval surfaces, the co-opted-fact-checking apparatus that flags or suppresses the named sources that captured-mediator does not want surfaced, captured-AI-knowledge interface that produces synthesised outputs whose underlying source-record the inquirer cannot examine. The procedural transparency that the cross-cultural recall-tradition dossiers as the procedural-accountability mechanism is broken. The procedural redundancy that the cross-cultural recall-tradition dossiers as the corruption-detection mechanism is broken. The named-transmitter procedural-accountability mechanism is broken — the mediator is not a named transmitter in the procedural sense; the compromised-mediator is an opaque algorithmic apparatus whose working procedures are not available for examination at the working level.

The procedural condition for sustained inquiry across generations is the procedural architecture of recall that the cross-cultural record dossiers. The procedural condition is older than the consolidation that Chapter 4 documents. The procedural condition does not require the contemporary captured-search-and-algorithmic-curation apparatus. The procedural condition required the named transmitter, the procedural redundancy, and the procedural transparency. The procedural condition is being weakened in the contemporary period not by the externalisation of the record per se but by the seized-mediator's procedural-accountability collapse.

The reader who has come this far is asked to keep this in mind. The procedural architecture of recall is older than the co-opted apparatus. The procedural architecture of recall survives the apparatus where individual working inquirers continue to hold the predecessor record in working memory, to maintain the procedural redundancy across named transmitters, and to insist on the procedural transparency of the recall-procedures the inquirer is willing to rely on. The procedural architecture is what the volume is written on behalf of, against captured displacement.

---


# Chapter 1.4. Judgement, Deliberation, and the Community of Inquirers


## I. What judgement is

If observation provides the empirical material and inquiry organises the disciplined question and recall maintains the transmission across generations, judgement is what the inquirer does when the record is incomplete, the inferences are contested, and the inquirer must nevertheless arrive at the working conclusion that subsequent inquiry will then check. Judgement is the procedural-cultural capacity to weigh the available evidence, to allow for what the evidence does not yet establish, to hold the conclusion provisionally enough that subsequent evidence can revise it, and to act on the conclusion in a manner that does not foreclose the inquiry.

Judgement is the procedural feature that the co-opted apparatus this volume documents has most systematically replaced. The captured apparatus does not ask the inquirer to exercise judgement on the available evidence. The co-opted apparatus tells the inquirer what the consensus has determined and asks the inquirer to accept it. The procedural difference is consequential. An inquirer exercising judgement on the available evidence may reach the same conclusion as the consensus or may reach a different conclusion. A consensus-accepting actor reaches only the consensus's conclusion, because the procedural capacity to reach a different conclusion has been removed.

The evidence covers what judgement was as a working procedure across the cross-cultural record before the consolidation Chapter 4 documents. The evidence covers the named procedural traditions of deliberative judgement, of legal-medical-empirical judgement under uncertainty, and of community-of-inquirers procedures for the working integration of multiple individual judgements into a working communal procedure. The evidence does not claim that pre-institutional judgement-traditions were uniformly correct. The record documents that judgement, as a working procedure under uncertainty, was practised across the cross-cultural record and is older than the consolidation that has progressively displaced it.


## II. The deliberative-judgement traditions

**The Athenian deliberative tradition.** The Athenian assembly (the *ekklesia*) and the council (the *boule*) operated as working deliberative bodies in the period of the radical democracy from roughly 461 BCE to 322 BCE. Mogens Herman Hansen's *The Athenian Democracy in the Age of Demosthenes* (Blackwell, 1991) dossiers the working procedural method. The *ekklesia* met roughly forty times per year on the Pnyx hill; any adult male citizen could attend, propose business, speak to the debate, and vote on the resulting decisions. The *boule* of 500 — fifty citizens from each of the ten tribes, selected by lot for a one-year term — set the agenda the *ekklesia* would consider, drafted the preliminary resolutions, and presided over the assembly's deliberation. The procedural method involved the explicit oral presentation of the available evidence on a given question, the explicit oral statement of the proposed conclusion, the explicit oral presentation of objections, the explicit oral defence of the proposal against the objections, and the working vote at the end. The Athenian deliberative procedure produced documented working judgements across the period — on military operations, on diplomatic relations, on legal cases at the *Heliaia* court, on public-works expenditure, on religious-festival organisation. The procedure was not uniformly correct; the Athenian assembly made working judgements that subsequent evidence demonstrated were wrong (the 415-413 BCE Sicilian expedition; the 406 BCE Arginusae trial in which the assembly executed six of the eight victorious generals on procedural-judgement grounds the subsequent evidence demonstrated were unsound; the 399 BCE conviction of Socrates). The procedure was a working deliberative-judgement procedure that the *demos* could revise on further evidence.

**The Roman senatorial tradition.** The Roman Senate operated as a working deliberative body across the Republican period from roughly 509 BCE to 27 BCE and continued, with reduced procedural function, into the Imperial period. The procedural method is dossiered in T. Robert S. Broughton's two-volume *The Magistrates of the Roman Republic* (American Philological Association, 1951-1952) and in Erich S. Gruen's *The Last Generation of the Roman Republic* (University of California Press, 1974). The Senate's procedural method involved the formal *sententiae* — the ordered statements of individual senatorial judgement on the question at hand, beginning with the senior ex-magistrates and proceeding through the named order — followed by the procedural vote. The procedural commitment is to the individual senatorial judgement on the question, dossiered in the working procedural record of the assembly. The procedural method was extensively imitated in the subsequent European parliamentary-deliberative tradition.

**The Iroquois Confederacy deliberative tradition.** The Haudenosaunee (Iroquois) Confederacy of the Five Nations (the Mohawk, Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga, and Seneca, joined by the Tuscarora in 1722 to form the Six Nations) operated as a working confederate deliberative institution from roughly the period 1142-1450 CE (the dating of the founding is contested in the record; Bruce E. Johansen and Barbara A. Mann's *Encyclopedia of the Haudenosaunee* (Greenwood, 2000) dossiers the dating debate) through the contemporary period. The Great Law of Peace — the *Kaianere'ko:wa* — codified the procedural method of confederate deliberation. The procedural method involved the working consensus of the named clan mothers, the named war chiefs, and the named sachems across the constituent nations, with the procedural commitment to consensus-by-deliberation rather than majority-vote, with the procedural rule that any sachem could declare the matter unresolved and require further deliberation, with the procedural rule that the women of the named clans held the position of the named sachem and could remove him if his judgement was unworthy of the position. The procedural method operated as a working deliberative-judgement procedure across roughly twenty-five generations from the foundation through the colonial-encounter period. Bruce E. Johansen's *Forgotten Founders: How the American Indian Helped Shape Democracy* (Gambit, 1982) and Donald A. Grinde Jr. and Bruce E. Johansen's *Exemplar of Liberty: Native America and the Evolution of Democracy* (UCLA American Indian Studies Center, 1991) dossier the evidentiary case for the influence of the Iroquois procedural method on the eighteenth-century European-American constitutional configuration.

**The African gerontocratic-deliberative traditions.** Across multiple named African traditions — the Akan, Igbo, Yoruba, Maasai, Luo, and named other configurations — working deliberative-judgement procedures operated through gerontocratic councils of named elders, with procedural methods involving the working consensus of the named elders, the working integration of consultative input from the broader community, and the working incorporation of the *palaver* tradition (the open communal deliberation in which any member of the community could speak to the question). Kwame Gyekye's *African Philosophical Thought: The Akan Conceptual Scheme* (Temple University Press, 1995) and the decades-long work of Kwasi Wiredu dossier the documented evidence of the Akan procedural method. The procedural methods operated as working deliberative-judgement procedures across centuries before the colonial-institutional disruption of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.

The cross-cultural pattern across the deliberative-judgement traditions is consistent. The procedural method involves the integration of multiple individual judgements through working procedural mechanisms that the community has developed for the integration. The procedural method does not claim infallibility. The procedural method does claim to produce working communal judgement under conditions of imperfect information.


## III. The legal-medical-empirical judgement traditions

Beyond the deliberative-political traditions, working judgement-under-uncertainty operated across the cross-cultural record in named legal, medical, and empirical-inquiry traditions in which the inquirer must arrive at the working conclusion despite incomplete evidence.

**The Roman legal-judgement tradition.** The Roman jurisprudential tradition from the late Republic through the Imperial period produced the procedural method of *responsa* — the documented working opinions of named jurists on contested legal questions. Tony Honoré's *Ulpian: Pioneer of Human Rights* (Oxford University Press, second edition 2002) dossiers the procedural method at the working level. The named jurist (Gaius, Ulpian, Papinian, Paulus, Modestinus — the so-called "Five Jurists" whose opinions were codified by the *Lex Citationum* of 426 CE) would produce a documented written opinion on the contested question, naming the applicable law, the relevant precedents, the working interpretive question, and the named jurist's working judgement. The procedural method dossiered the inquirer's working judgement in such detail that subsequent jurists could examine the procedure by which the working judgement was reached and could revise the working judgement on further evidence or further argument. The Justinianic codification (the *Corpus Iuris Civilis* of 529-534 CE) preserved the procedural record and transmitted it to the subsequent European legal tradition.

**The Hippocratic medical-judgement tradition.** Chapter 1.2 dossiered the Hippocratic clinical-observation tradition. The corresponding procedural commitment is the working medical judgement under uncertainty. The Hippocratic *Aphorisms* (Book I, in W. H. S. Jones's Loeb edition 1923) opened with the procedural statement: "Life is short, the art long, opportunity fleeting, experience perilous, judgement difficult. The physician must not only do what is right himself, but must make the patient, the attendants, and externals cooperate." The procedural commitment is that the physician must exercise working judgement on the available evidence of the named case, recognising that the evidence is incomplete, that the inferences are uncertain, and that the physician's working judgement is what the case requires. The procedural commitment continued in the Galenic tradition (Galen's *De Methodo Medendi,* second century CE), in the Islamic medical tradition under Ibn Sīnā's *Qānūn fī al-Ṭibb* (*Canon of Medicine,* completed c. 1025), and in the broader cross-cultural medical record across two thousand years.

**The Common-law judgement tradition.** The English common-law tradition from roughly the twelfth century onward — dossiered in J. H. Baker's *An Introduction to English Legal History* (LexisNexis, fourth edition 2002) and Frederic William Maitland's *The Forms of Action at Common Law* (Cambridge University Press, 1909, posthumously published) — operates through the procedural method of the named judge reasoning from the named precedent to the working judgement on the named case. The procedural method involves the documented working judgement of the named judge, the documented reasoning by which the working judgement was reached, the named precedents on which the working judgement rests, and the procedural commitment that subsequent judges may distinguish, follow, or overturn the working judgement on further argument and further evidence. The procedural method has operated as a working judgement-under-uncertainty tradition across roughly eight hundred years across multiple jurisdictions descended from the English common-law tradition.

**The Talmudic legal-judgement tradition.** Chapter 1.2 dossiered the Talmudic disputation-procedure. The corresponding procedural commitment is the working halakhic judgement on the named case. The procedural method involves the named *posek* — the working halakhic decisor — examining the relevant Talmudic precedent, the documented responsa of named earlier *poskim,* and the working features of the named case to arrive at the working judgement. The procedural record dossiers the *posek's* reasoning in the *teshuvah* — the written responsum — such that subsequent *poskim* can examine the procedure and revise the working judgement on further argument. The procedural method has operated across roughly fifteen centuries from the redaction of the Talmud through the contemporary period.

The cross-cultural pattern across the legal-medical-empirical judgement traditions is consistent. The procedural method involves the named inquirer producing a documented working judgement on the available evidence, dossiering the reasoning in sufficient detail that subsequent inquirers can examine the procedure, and accepting the procedural commitment that the working judgement may be revised on further evidence. The procedural method does not require institutional consolidation of the modern type. The procedural method is older than the consolidation.


## IV. The community-of-inquirers procedural commitment

Beyond the individual judgement of the named inquirer, the cross-cultural record dossiers working procedural mechanisms for the integration of multiple individual judgements into a working communal judgement.

**The Talmudic *machloket* preservation.** Chapter 1.2 dossiered the Talmudic procedural commitment to preserve the rejected position alongside the accepted position. The procedural commitment operates as a working community-of-inquirers procedure: the working communal judgement at any given period (the *halakhah* — the working binding interpretation) is documented alongside the rejected alternative positions, such that the working communal judgement can be revisited by subsequent inquirers if the underlying conditions change or further evidence emerges. The community-of-inquirers procedure does not claim to settle the question for all time. The community-of-inquirers procedure produces the working communal judgement for the present period while preserving the procedural capacity to revise the working judgement on further evidence.

**The Buddhist *sangha* procedural deliberation.** The Buddhist monastic community (the *sangha*) operated working procedural mechanisms for the resolution of contested questions within the community across roughly 2,500 years. The Vinaya texts — the monastic-disciplinary canon — dossier the procedural methods for the resolution of disputes among members of the *sangha,* for the integration of multiple judgements on procedural questions, and for the working communal judgement on contested doctrinal questions. The four Buddhist councils — the First Council at Rājagṛha c. 483 BCE, the Second Council at Vaiśālī c. 383 BCE, the Third Council at Pāṭaliputra c. 250 BCE under Aśoka, and the Fourth Council in the first or second century CE (with parallel later councils in the Theravāda and Mahāyāna traditions) — operated as working communal deliberative bodies that produced the working communal judgement on contested doctrinal and procedural questions. André Bareau's *Les premiers conciles bouddhiques* (Presses universitaires de France, 1955) and Charles Prebish's "A Review of Scholarship on the Buddhist Councils" (*Journal of Asian Studies,* 33(2), 1974) dossier the evidence.

**The Confucian scholarly-community procedural deliberation.** The Confucian scholarly tradition across roughly two and a half millennia operated working procedural mechanisms for the resolution of contested interpretive questions within the scholarly community. The procedural mechanisms involved the named commentator, the documented working interpretation, the named opposing commentator, the documented opposing interpretation, the named subsequent commentator who arbitrated between them, the documented working communal interpretation that the named scholarly community accepted for the period. The procedural record is preserved in the layered commentaries on the Confucian classics — the original text, the early commentary (e.g., Zheng Xuan's late-Han commentaries c. 200 CE), the medieval commentary (Kong Yingda's *Wujing Zhengyi* of 642 CE), the Song-dynasty commentary (Zhu Xi's *Sishu Zhangju Jizhu* of c. 1190 CE), the Qing-dynasty commentary (the *kaozheng* documentary-critical commentaries from the late seventeenth through nineteenth centuries). The layered commentary structure dossiers the working communal procedural-judgement record across the centuries.

The pattern across the cross-cultural community-of-inquirers traditions is consistent. The community-of-inquirers operates working procedural mechanisms for the integration of multiple individual judgements into a working communal judgement at the present period, while preserving the procedural capacity to revise the working judgement on further evidence. The procedural mechanisms involve the named participants, the documented working procedure, the procedural transparency, and the procedural commitment to revision on further evidence.
