The Fauci's-Daszak architecture is the operational anchor of the compromised-public-health apparatus's positioning on the lab-leak hypothesis across the documented 2020-2022 period. **The contemporary US architecture that characterised the lab-leak hypothesis as "discredited" was, in operational substance, the architecture funded by and operationally coordinated through the same federal-government funding stream that had funded the Wuhan-Institute-of-Virology bat-coronavirus research.** The conflict of interest at Fauci's-NIAID-EcoHealth-Wuhan level was the architecture that the press sector's positioning across the documented 2020-2022 period elided.

### The June 2024 NIH debarment

The documented **NIH debarment of EcoHealth Alliance** was issued on **17 May 2024** following the HHS Office of the Inspector General investigation and the House Select Subcommittee on the Coronavirus Pandemic investigation findings. The debarment documented that EcoHealth Alliance had violated documented NIH grant-compliance requirements throughout the post-2014 period in relation to the Wuhan-Institute-of-Virology research collaboration. The HHS's debarment was the consequence of the contemporary US federal-government record on the EcoHealth's Alliance positioning during the post-2020 period.

The consequence of the HHS debarment is that the contemporary US federal-government positioning, across the 2024-2025 transition, reversed the prior decade of positioning on the EcoHealth's Alliance record. **The entity that had funded and operationally coordinated the Wuhan-Institute-of-Virology bat-coronavirus research since the post-2014 period was, by the 17 May 2024 federal-government action, debarred from federal-government grant funding at the level.**


## V. The 2024-2026 repositioning

The post-November-2024 contemporary US captured-public-health repositioning has produced the most rapid contemporary US federal-government public-health repositioning in the record. The evidence covers the documented repositioning.

### The Trump II administration public-health appointments

**Robert F. Kennedy Jr.** was confirmed as **HHS Secretary on 13 February 2025** by a Senate vote of 52-48. Kennedy's record since the post-2011 period at Children's Health Defense, the Kennedy's positioning on contemporary US vaccine-policy questions, the Kennedy's April 2023 Democratic presidential candidacy, the Kennedy's October 2023 independent presidential candidacy, and the Kennedy's August 2024 endorsement of Trump together anchor the position. **Kennedy's HHS's Secretary positioning has produced actions across the 2025-2026 period including:** the restructuring of the Vaccine Adverse Event Reporting System reporting infrastructure, the restructuring of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP), the changes to the CDC vaccine-recommendation positioning, the restructuring of the pharmaceutical-industry user-fee architecture at the working level, and the broader contemporary HHS repositioning across the sectoral architecture of HHS-overseen agencies.

**Jay Bhattacharya** was sworn in as **NIH Director on 1 April 2025** as the 18th NIH Director. Bhattacharya's record across the Stanford School of Medicine professorship, the Great Barrington Declaration co-authorship of 4 October 2020, the contested-policy positioning across the COVID-19 period, and the post-2022 academic-research positioning anchor the NIH Director appointment. **Bhattacharya's documented NIH Director positioning has produced actions across the 2025-2026 period including:** the NIH research-grant prioritisation repositioning, the restructuring of the gain-of-function research oversight (the federal moratorium on gain-of-function research on potential pandemic pathogens was reinstated in May 2025 under federal-government action), and the contemporary NIH repositioning at the level.

**Martin Makary** was sworn in as **FDA Commissioner on 1 April 2025**. Makary's record across the Johns Hopkins School of Medicine professorship and the contemporary contested-policy positioning across the COVID-19 period anchor the FDA Commissioner appointment. **Makary's documented FDA Commissioner positioning has produced actions across the 2025-2026 period including:** the FDA drug-approval-process repositioning, the restructuring of the FDA Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research operational positioning under Makary's appointment of **Vinay Prasad** as Director from May 2025, and the broader contemporary FDA repositioning.

### The WHO withdrawal and the gain-of-function moratorium

The documented **20 January 2025 Trump executive order** announced US withdrawal from the WHO with the 12-month notice period through January 2026. The WHO withdrawal was the contemporary US federal-government reversal of the post-1948 US-WHO architecture at the working level.

The gain-of-function research moratorium was reinstated in May 2025 under federal-government action. The documented moratorium operates against the pre-2024 contemporary US federal-government gain-of-function research positioning at the level.

### The 2024-2026 reversal summary

The 2024-2026 reversal at the contemporary US federal-government public-health level is the most rapid contemporary US federal-government public-health repositioning in the record. **The "fringe epidemiologists" Collins characterised in his 8 October 2020 directive to Fauci are, by April 2025, the NIH Director and the FDA Commissioner's senior FDA appointee.** The architecture that characterised the contested-policy positions as "misinformation," "fringe," "dangerous," or "discredited" across the documented 2020-2024 period has been reversed at the contemporary US federal-government level in the post-November-2024 transition.

The volume documents the reversal. The volume does not adjudicate whether the contested positions on the underlying public-health questions are correct on the documented scientific merits. The reader can follow every source on the underlying empirical questions. **What the volume documents is that the co-opted-public-health apparatus's consensus across the documented 2020-2024 period operated against the contested-policy figures whose documented positions have, across the 2024-2026 reversal, been elevated to the documented senior US federal-government positions in the contemporary period.**


## VI. The chapter's conclusion

The record has dossiered the seized public-health apparatus across its founding, its named senior figures, the documented funding architecture, the contested-cases record of the COVID-19 period, the Daszak's-EcoHealth-Wuhan operator-level record, and the 2024-2026 repositioning. The record has named the record at the level the evidence permits.

The structural position the chapter establishes: **captured public-health apparatus is the sector of the knowledge sectoral architecture whose determinations had the most direct contact with the most human bodies in the shortest time across the documented 2020-2023 period.** The contested cases. The lab-leak case, the Great Barrington Declaration case, the natural-immunity case, the vaccine-and-transmission case, the ivermectin/hydroxychloroquine cases, the mask-mandate cases — are the record on which the structural counter-claim rests. The co-opted apparatus issued consensus across multiple contested questions; the consensus was contradicted in the post-2022 record at multiple operational levels; the contested-policy figures who had articulated the contested-policy positions across the documented 2020-2022 period have, by the 2024-2026 reversal, been elevated to the documented senior US federal-government positions.

The volume does not adjudicate the disputed questions on the underlying scientific merits. **The volume traces the record and names what the compromised apparatus did.** Check the documentation on the contested questions.

The section that follows dossiers the co-opted climate-policy apparatus. The reader proceeds from the co-opted public-health sector to the climate-policy sector.

# Chapter 8. The co-opted climate-Policy Apparatus

The captured climate-policy apparatus is the sector of captured-knowledge architecture in which the disputed question — whether anthropogenic CO₂ emissions are the dominant driver of contemporary observed climate change, on what timescales, with what magnitude, and with what policy implications — has been operationally pre-resolved at the consensus level for purposes that the volume documents below. The apparatus's determinations have, across the post-1988 period, shaped the contemporary global energy-and-industrial-policy architecture at a scale exceeding all other captured sectors except captured public-health apparatus.

The documentation covers the apparatus across five operating dimensions. **First**, the founding period and the consolidation that produced the IPCC, the named climate-research architecture, and the funding flows that constructed both. **Second**, the Climategate / hockey-stick contested cases of the 2009-2011 period and the response. **Third**, the operator-level record at the named-senior-figure level — Hansen, Mann, Schmidt, Trenberth, the IPCC senior authors. **Fourth**, the contemporary peer-review-suppression record and the Mann-v.-Steyn litigation outcome. **Fifth**, the 2024-2026 repositioning at the contemporary US federal-government climate-policy level and the ESG-finance exit.

The procedural standard from Chapters 1 through 7 holds. The evidence does not adjudicate the underlying empirical questions on climate science at the level of the physical mechanisms, the climate-sensitivity parameter, the magnitude of anthropogenic forcing, the historical-temperature reconstructions, the projections of future warming, or the cost-benefit analysis of mitigation versus adaptation policies. **The record covers the architecture, the operator-level record, the contested cases where the consensus operated through suppression of contested positions, and the 2024-2026 repositioning.** Every claim is auditable on the underlying empirical questions.


## I. Founding and pre-2009 architecture

The contemporary climate-policy apparatus has a founding lineage from the post-1957 International Geophysical Year through the post-1988 consolidation. The record names the anchors before turning to the contemporary apparatus.

### The post-1957 founding period

The International Geophysical Year (1 July 1957 - 31 December 1958) anchored the contemporary global-atmospheric-research architecture at the founding period. The **Charles David Keeling** atmospheric CO₂ measurement program at the Mauna Loa Observatory began in **March 1958** as one of the IGY's research initiatives; the resulting "Keeling Curve" of atmospheric CO₂ concentrations is the canonical contemporary empirical anchor of the CO₂-concentration record. Keeling's measurements, continued by his son Ralph Keeling after Charles's death in 2005, are the primary contemporary CO₂-concentration documentary record at the working level. The Keeling Curve has atmospheric CO₂ rising from roughly 315 parts per million in 1958 to roughly 425 ppm by 2024 — a roughly 35% increase across the 66-year documentary record.

The post-1965 US positioning on the CO₂ record traces to the **Lyndon Johnson administration's "Restoring the Quality of Our Environment" report** of November 1965, in which the President's Science Advisory Committee characterised the CO₂ rise as "a continuing major and observable phenomenon." The report was the first US presidential-administration positioning on the anthropogenic-CO₂ question. The **Jule Charney** report of 1979 ("Carbon Dioxide and Climate: A Scientific Assessment," National Academy of Sciences) anchored the canonical contemporary US scientific-institution positioning on the climate-sensitivity parameter at the founding period. The Charney sensitivity estimate of 1.5-4.5°C per doubling of atmospheric CO₂ has anchored the contemporary scientific-institution positioning during the post-1979 period.

### The founding of the IPCC

The **Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change** (IPCC) was founded in **1988** through joint action by the **World Meteorological Organization** (WMO) and the **United Nations Environment Programme** (UNEP). The IPCC's founding positioning was to provide periodic comprehensive assessments of the scientific literature on climate change for the use of contemporary global policymakers. The IPCC has, throughout the post-1988 period, produced six Assessment Reports (AR1 1990, AR2 1995, AR3 2001, AR4 2007, AR5 2013-2014, AR6 2021-2023) plus numerous Special Reports and Methodology Reports.

The IPCC architecture operates through three Working Groups. **Working Group I** assesses the physical science basis of climate change; **Working Group II** assesses impacts, adaptation, and vulnerability; **Working Group III** assesses mitigation. The IPCC AR6 Working Group I report (released August 2021) characterised the attribution of contemporary observed warming to human influence as "unequivocal." The IPCC AR6 Synthesis Report (released March 2023) characterised the contemporary trajectory of warming as "1.1°C above pre-industrial levels" with "human-caused emissions" as the dominant driver.

The IPCC's architecture has produced the contemporary global scientific-consensus reference document on the anthropogenic-climate-change question. **The reports anchor the contemporary global climate-policy architecture at the reference level.** The contemporary global climate-policy negotiations — the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC, established 1992), the Kyoto Protocol (1997, in force from 2005), the Paris Agreement (adopted December 2015, in force from November 2016) — have operated within the IPCC scientific-consensus framework.

### The James Hansen position

**James Hansen**'s positioning anchored the contemporary US scientific-institution public-positioning on the anthropogenic-climate-change question since the post-1988 period. Hansen served as Director of the **NASA Goddard Institute for Space Studies** (GISS) from 1981 through his April 2013 retirement. **Hansen's 23 June 1988 testimony before the US Senate Committee on Energy and Natural Resources**, chaired by Senator Timothy Wirth, was the US public-positioning event that anchored the contemporary US public attention on the anthropogenic-climate-change question. Hansen's testimony characterised the contemporary observed warming as "99 percent certain" to be the consequence of anthropogenic factors at the level.

The context of the Hansen testimony was contested at the contemporary documentary level. Senator Tim Wirth, in subsequent interviews including the PBS Frontline interview of 2007, characterised the hearing scheduling as deliberately operationally orchestrated: **"We called the Weather Bureau and found out what historically was the hottest day of the summer. Well, it was June 6th or June 9th or whatever it was, so we scheduled the hearing that day, and bingo: it was the hottest day on record in Washington, or close to it. And we went in the night before and opened all the windows, I will admit, right? So that the air conditioning wasn't working inside the room and so when the, when the hearing occurred there was not only bliss, which is television cameras in double figures, but it was really hot."** The Wirth's statement was the anchor of the contemporary contested-positioning on the Hansen's testimony's public-relations construction.

Hansen's post-1988 positioning across his 25 remaining years at NASA GISS continued the public-positioning on the anthropogenic-climate-change question. The Hansen's public-positioning has, since the post-2008 period, articulated positions on climate-policy interventions including advocacy for carbon-fee-and-dividend architecture, opposition to coal-fired power generation, and advocacy for nuclear-power generation as a climate-mitigation mechanism. The Hansen's positioning on nuclear power has been contested at the working level by contemporary environmental-organisation positions that have, in the post-1979 Three Mile Island period, articulated opposition to nuclear power that the Hansen's positioning operates against.

### The fossil-fuel-industry funding architecture

The current disputed positionitioning on the climate-policy question has produced a fossil-fuel-industry counter-positioning architecture throughout the post-1989 period. The evidence names the record.

The **Global Climate Coalition** (GCC) operated from 1989 to 2002 as the principal contemporary US fossil-fuel-industry coordinating body opposing US climate-policy interventions. The GCC's named participating corporations across its operational period included **ExxonMobil** (and predecessor entities Exxon and Mobil), Royal Dutch Shell, BP, Texaco, Chevron, Amoco, the American Petroleum Institute, the National Coal Association, the National Mining Association, the Edison Electric Institute, and named other fossil-fuel-industry positions. The GCC's positioning across the 1989-2002 period was that the climate-science consensus on the anthropogenic-climate-change question was contested at the contemporary scientific-research literature level and that the US climate-policy interventions were not warranted on the contested basis.

The GCC's dissolution in 2002 followed the withdrawal of multiple major corporate members (BP withdrew 1997, Shell withdrew 1998, Ford withdrew 1999, DaimlerChrysler withdrew 1999, Texaco withdrew 1999, General Motors withdrew 2000) and the repositioning of the contemporary US fossil-fuel-industry positioning on the climate-policy question during the post-2000 period.

The ExxonMobil's internal-research record since the post-1977 period has been through subsequent investigative reporting (the 16 September 2015 *InsideClimate News* investigation; the 2015-2017 academic-research literature including Geoffrey Supran and Naomi Oreskes's Harvard publications). The internal-research record that ExxonMobil's internal scientists had, since the post-1977 period, produced research that was operationally aligned with the contemporary scientific-consensus position on the anthropogenic-climate-change question. **The present contested positionitioning is that the internal-research record contradicted the public-positioning ExxonMobil articulated at the level across the 1989-2002 period.** The contemporary US state attorneys general litigation against ExxonMobil and named other contemporary fossil-fuel-industry corporations. Including the New York Attorney General litigation under Eric Schneiderman, the Massachusetts Attorney General litigation under Maura Healey, and named other state-level litigations across the 2015-2024 period — has operated against the ExxonMobil's contemporary public-positioning record.

### The contemporary climate-philanthropy funding architecture

The contemporary climate-philanthropy funding architecture operates as the counterweight to the fossil-fuel-industry contested-positioning. The record names the major contemporary climate-philanthropy positions.

The **Rockefeller Brothers Fund** announced divestment from fossil-fuel positions in September 2014. The Rockefeller Brothers Fund position was the inflection point at which the contemporary global climate-divestment movement anchored its positioning at the major US foundation level. **Cross-reference Chapter 5 § III** on the Rockefeller Brothers Fund positioning in the post-1940 period.

The **Bloomberg Philanthropies** climate-funding architecture has produced major contemporary contributions in the post-2010 period. The Bloomberg's climate-funding includes the **"Beyond Coal" campaign** (with cumulative Bloomberg's Philanthropies contributions exceeding $500 million across the post-2011 period to the Sierra Club and named other organisations operating against contemporary US coal-fired power generation) and named other Bloomberg's climate-philanthropy positions.

The **William and Flora Hewlett Foundation**, the **ClimateWorks Foundation**, the **Energy Foundation**, the **Climate Imperative Foundation**, the **Bezos Earth Fund** (founded 2020 with $10 billion in announced commitment from Jeff Bezos), and named other contemporary climate-philanthropy positions together constitute the contemporary climate-philanthropy funding architecture at the level. The cumulative climate-philanthropy contributions across the contemporary US major foundations have, during the post-2010 period, produced contributions at the multi-tens-of-billions cumulative dollar scale.

The key finding: **the contemporary climate-policy debate operates between a fossil-fuel-industry contested-positioning architecture (the GCC successor entities, the named industry associations, the industry-funded contemporary contested-positioning research institutions) and a climate-philanthropy funding architecture (the major US foundations, the Bezos's Earth Fund, the contemporary climate-philanthropy architecture) at the multi-tens-of-billions cumulative dollar scale on each side**. The contested-positioning is funded; the consensus-positioning is funded. The volume does not adjudicate which positioning is correct on the underlying empirical questions. The volume documents the funding architecture on both sides.


## II. Climategate and the Mann hockey-stick contested cases

The contested record on the contemporary climate-policy question contains a specific contested case that operationally anchors the contemporary contested-positioning on the consensus. What follows covers the case.

### The Mann hockey-stick paper

**Michael E. Mann** (then a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Massachusetts Amherst; subsequently a Pennsylvania State University faculty member from 1999; subsequently a University of Pennsylvania faculty member from 2022) co-authored, with Raymond S. Bradley and Malcolm K. Hughes, the climate-reconstruction papers **MBH98** ("Global-scale temperature patterns and climate forcing over the past six centuries," *Nature*, April 1998) and **MBH99** ("Northern Hemisphere temperatures during the past millennium," *Geophysical Research Letters*, March 1999). The MBH98/99 papers produced the temperature reconstruction that subsequently became known as **"the hockey stick"** — a graph showing relatively stable Northern Hemisphere temperatures across the 1000-1900 period followed by a sharp 20th-century temperature increase.

The MBH98/99 hockey-stick graph was prominently featured in the **IPCC Third Assessment Report** (2001), in which the hockey stick was the headline graphic of the Working Group I Summary for Policymakers. The IPCC TAR positioning characterised the hockey-stick reconstruction as evidence that the contemporary 20th-century warming was unprecedented across the prior thousand years and that the anthropogenic-climate-change consensus rested on the multi-proxy reconstruction at the working level.

The hockey-stick reconstruction was subsequently contested in the academic-research literature. **Stephen McIntyre** (a Canadian mining-industry researcher operating as an independent statistical researcher) and **Ross McKitrick** (a University of Guelph economist) published critical analyses of the MBH98/99 methodology in 2003 and 2005. The McIntyre-McKitrick analyses that the MBH98/99 principal-components-analysis methodology had a the mathematical defect that produced hockey-stick-shaped outputs even when the input data was random noise. The McIntyre's-McKitrick papers — including "Corrections to the Mann et al. (1998) Proxy Data Base and Northern Hemispheric Average Temperature Series" (*Energy and Environment*, 2003) and "Hockey sticks, principal components, and spurious significance" (*Geophysical Research Letters*, 2005) — anchored the contested position on the hockey-stick reconstruction at the academic-research literature level.

The response to the McIntyre-McKitrick critiques produced the **Wegman Report** (the report of the National Research Council ad hoc Committee on Surface Temperature Reconstructions for the Last 2,000 Years, chaired by **Edward Wegman** of George Mason University, commissioned by the US House Committee on Energy and Commerce under Chair Joe Barton in 2006). The Wegman's Report findings included validation of the McIntyre-McKitrick statistical critiques. The National Academy of Sciences report of June 2006 ("Surface Temperature Reconstructions for the Last 2,000 Years") characterised the MBH98/99 reconstruction as having "less confidence" for the period prior to 1600 than the original MBH98/99 publication had articulated.

The import of the hockey-stick contested case is that the IPCC TAR positioning anchored the contemporary global climate-policy architecture on a temperature reconstruction whose methodology was subsequently to have statistical defects at the academic-research literature level. The IPCC AR4 (2007) and AR5 (2013) positioning subsequently included modified hockey-stick reconstructions that operated within the academic-research literature critique. **The contemporary positioning on the contested case has been that the hockey-stick result is as meaningfully confirmed by the subsequent academic-research literature, even where the original MBH98/99 methodology was as defective.** The ongoing contested postureitioning on the case continues to be contested at the academic-research literature level.

### Climategate

The **Climategate** disclosure was the November 2009 release of roughly 1,000 internal emails from the **Climatic Research Unit** (CRU) at the **University of East Anglia** (UEA) in the United Kingdom. The emails were obtained through unauthorised access to the CRU's email server and released onto the public internet in the immediate pre-COP15 (Copenhagen Climate Change Conference, December 2009) period. The disclosed emails internal correspondence among the contemporary major climate-research institutional senior figures including **Phil Jones** (CRU Director), **Michael E. Mann** (Penn State), **Keith Briffa** (CRU), **Kevin Trenberth** (National Center for Atmospheric Research), **Tom Wigley** (NCAR), and named other senior figures.

The Climategate emails contained specific the passages that anchored the contemporary contested-positioning on the climate-policy consensus. The emails included:

- **The Phil Jones "Mike's Nature trick" email of 16 November 1999**, which referenced "the trick of adding in the real temps to each series for the last 20 years (i.e. from 1981 onwards) and from 1961 for Keith's [Briffa's] to hide the decline." The "trick" and "hide the decline" passages anchored the contemporary contested-positioning on the multi-proxy reconstruction methodology. The contested-positioning characterised the "trick" and "hide the decline" passages as evidence of methodological manipulation; the response characterised the passages as referring to the mathematical procedure for joining instrumental temperature records to tree-ring proxy records and the "decline" as referring to the "divergence problem" in late-20th-century tree-ring records.
- **The Phil Jones email of 8 July 2004**, referring to two contested-positioning papers (by McKitrick and Pat Michaels) that were under consideration for inclusion in the IPCC AR4: "I can't see either of these papers being in the next IPCC report. Kevin [Trenberth] and I will keep them out somehow — even if we have to redefine what the peer-review literature is!" The passage anchored the contemporary contested-positioning on IPCC literature-inclusion decisions.
- **The Phil Jones email of 21 January 2005**, in which Jones discussed Freedom of Information Act requests for the CRU's temperature data: "If they ever hear there is a Freedom of Information Act now in the UK, I think I'll delete the file rather than send to anyone." The passage anchored the contemporary contested-positioning on the data-disclosure practices at the major contemporary climate-research level.
- Named additional the passages on IPCC-process internal coordination, peer-review positioning, institutional-funding considerations, and named other topics.

The response to Climategate operated through subsequent investigations. The Penn State investigation cleared Mann of research misconduct in two separate 2010 inquiries. The UK House of Commons Science and Technology Committee inquiry of March 2010 cleared Phil Jones of misconduct. The Muir Russell Independent Climate Change Email Review of July 2010 cleared the CRU's researchers of manipulation of scientific evidence. The Oxburgh Panel report of April 2010 cleared the CRU's researchers of misconduct.

The disputed contemporary positionitioning on the response is that the investigations operated within the contemporary climate-research architecture — that the investigations were institutionally-conducted by institutions and individuals with interests in the climate-research architecture's positioning. **The response cleared the researchers of misconduct; the contested-positioning maintains that the passages anchor a operating posture that research-process integrity at the contemporary major climate-research level had been compromised at the level prior to the unauthorised disclosure.**

The true structural significance of Climategate is that the contemporary major climate-research architecture's operational positioning on peer-review, the data-disclosure, and IPCC-process integrity was to operate in ways that the public-positioning had not previously articulated. **The volume documents the Climategate disclosure and the response. The volume does not adjudicate whether the passages establish research-process misconduct on the underlying empirical merits.** The reader's own judgment applies on the contested-positioning.


## III. The Mann v. Steyn / Simberg litigation

The active contested positionitioning on the hockey-stick case produced the major US contemporary climate-policy litigation. The evidence covers the litigation.

### The litigation history

The **Mann v. National Review, Competitive Enterprise Institute, Steyn, and Simberg** litigation was filed by Michael E. Mann in the **DC Superior Court on 22 October 2012**. The the complaint named four defendants: the **National Review** (the contemporary US conservative-positioned publication), the **Competitive Enterprise Institute** (the contemporary US contested-positioning think tank), **Mark Steyn** (a contemporary US contested-positioning columnist), and **Rand Simberg** (a Competitive Enterprise Institute fellow). The the complaint alleged defamation in connection with contemporary public-positioning statements published by the defendants characterising the Mann's hockey-stick research.

The specific statements at issue included Simberg's 13 July 2012 Competitive Enterprise Institute blog post characterising Mann as "the Jerry Sandusky of climate science" who had "molested and tortured data" and Steyn's 15 July 2012 National Review online post characterising the Mann research as "fraudulent." The Mann's complaint alleged the statements were defamatory and sought monetary damages.

The litigation continued across the 2012-2024 period through motions for dismissal, appeals to the DC Court of Appeals, and procedural delays. The National Review and the Competitive Enterprise Institute were dismissed from the case across the period; the litigation continued against Steyn and Simberg individually.

### The 8 February 2024 verdict

The the jury trial in the Mann's v. Steyn / Simberg case concluded in the DC Superior Court on **8 February 2024**. The the jury verdict found Steyn and Simberg liable for defamation. **The the jury awarded Mann $1 in compensatory damages, $1 in compensatory damages from each defendant, $1,000 in punitive damages against Simberg, and $1,000,000 in punitive damages against Steyn.** The punitive-damages award against Steyn was the outcome of the contested case at the US federal-court level.

The contested current positionitioning on the verdict was contested at the working level. The post-verdict appeals process is ongoing across the 2024-2026 period. The Steyn's defense's argument at trial had been that the characterisation of the Mann's research as "fraudulent" was the Steyn's protected speech under the First Amendment, that the characterisation operated within the academic-research literature critique of the MBH98/99 methodology, and that the Mann's research was a public-figure matter for which the *New York Times* v. Sullivan standard required actual-malice proof for defamation liability.

What the Mann's v. Steyn litigation is the case in which the contemporary US federal-court architecture the contemporary contested-positioning on the hockey-stick reconstruction at the defamation-litigation operational level. **The outcome of the twelve-year litigation was a $1 million punitive-damages award against Mark Steyn for characterisation of the Mann's research as "fraudulent."** The academic-research literature critique of the MBH98/99 methodology. Which the McIntyre's-McKitrick analyses had articulated at the academic-research literature level throughout the post-2003 period — continued to be the contemporary contested-positioning on the case since the post-verdict period.


## IV. The contemporary peer-review-suppression record

The current operational positionitioning on the consensus on climate-policy includes cases in which the contemporary academic-research literature on contested climate-research questions has been to operate through peer-review-suppression mechanisms. The evidence names selected cases.

### The Soon-Baliunas case

**Willie Soon** (Harvard-Smithsonian Center for Astrophysics) and **Sallie Baliunas** (Harvard-Smithsonian Center for Astrophysics) published "Proxy climatic and environmental changes of the past 1000 years" in *Climate Research* in 2003. The Soon's-Baliunas paper reviewed multi-proxy temperature reconstructions and characterised the Medieval Warm Period and the Little Ice Age as global phenomena that the contemporary IPCC TAR positioning had not adequately characterised. The publication produced response: the Editor-in-Chief Hans von Storch and the Managing Editor Clare Goodess resigned from *Climate Research* in protest of the publication. The Soon's-Baliunas case anchored the contemporary contested-positioning on peer-review-process positioning at the major contemporary climate-research journal level.

### The Lennart Bengtsson case

**Lennart Bengtsson** (Swedish meteorologist; former Director of the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts; former Director of the Max Planck Institute for Meteorology) joined the Global Warming Policy Foundation (GWPF, the contemporary UK contested-positioning think tank founded by Nigel Lawson in November 2009) advisory board in 2014. The Bengtsson's resignation from the GWPF advisory board occurred two weeks after the appointment following pressure from contemporary climate-research community senior figures. **Bengtsson's resignation statement** characterised the contemporary climate-research community positioning: "It is a situation that reminds me about the time of McCarthy. I would never have expected anything similar in such an original peaceful community as meteorology. Apparently it has been transformed in recent years."

The Bengtsson's case anchored the contemporary contested-positioning on academic-research-community positioning on contested positions on the climate-policy question. **The contemporary academic-research literature on the contested climate-research questions has, since the post-2009 period, been to operate through positioning that contested positions face barriers to academic-research-literature publication that consensus-positioning positions do not face.**

### The 97% consensus question

The present disputed stanceitioning on the consensus has included contestation of the "97% consensus" claim that has been the contemporary US public-positioning anchor on the anthropogenic-climate-change question. The "97% consensus" claim traces to the **John Cook et al. paper "Quantifying the consensus on anthropogenic global warming in the scientific literature"** published in *Environmental Research Letters* in May 2013. The Cook's et al. paper analysed roughly 12,000 contemporary climate-research-literature paper abstracts and characterised roughly 97% of the abstracts that took a position on the anthropogenic-climate-change question as supporting the anthropogenic-climate-change positioning.

The ongoing disputeitioning on the "97% consensus" claim has characterised the Cook's et al. methodology as documenting the contemporary academic-research-literature positioning at the level but as not documenting the contemporary academic-research-community consensus on the climate-sensitivity parameter. The magnitude of anthropogenic forcing, the projections of future warming, the cost-benefit analysis of mitigation versus adaptation policies, or the other contested questions at the working level. **The contemporary contested-positioning is that the "97% consensus" claim characterises the academic-research literature position on a narrowly-defined question that does not address the contemporary contested climate-policy questions at the level.**

The volume documents the Cook's et al. publication and the contemporary contested-positioning. The volume does not adjudicate the underlying contested questions on climate science. The audit trail is open.


## V. The 2024-2026 repositioning

The post-November-2024 contemporary US captured-climate-policy repositioning has produced major contemporary reversals at the contemporary US federal-government climate-policy level. The documentation covers the repositioning.

### The Trump II administration climate-policy reversals

The **20 January 2025 Trump executive order** "Putting America First in International Environmental Agreements" announced US withdrawal from the Paris Agreement, the second US withdrawal from the Paris Agreement after the 2017-2020 Trump I administration withdrawal that was subsequently reversed by the Biden administration on 20 January 2021. The 20 January 2025 withdrawal operates within the Paris Agreement's one-year notice period.

The Trump II administration climate-policy reversals across the 2025 period included the **rescission of the Biden administration's "social cost of carbon" calculations**, the **Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) repositioning** including operational restrictions on clean-energy tax-credit architecture. The **EPA repositioning** under EPA Administrator **Lee Zeldin** (sworn in 29 January 2025), the **NOAA repositioning**, the **National Climate Assessment repositioning**, and named other climate-policy reversals at the contemporary US federal-government level.

The **23 January 2025 Trump executive order "Unleashing American Energy"** rescinded the Biden Executive Order 14008 (Tackling the Climate Crisis at Home and Abroad) and rescinded multiple Biden administration climate-related executive orders at the working level. The executive order operationally repositioned the US federal-government energy-and-climate architecture at the level.

### The ESG-finance exit

The contemporary US major asset-manager and bank architecture has produced the 2024-2025 exit from the contemporary ESG-finance architecture. The exits at the working level:

- **BlackRock** announced the exit from the **Net Zero Asset Managers initiative (NZAM)** on **9 January 2025**. BlackRock's positioning on the ESG-investment architecture has been characterised by CEO Larry Fink in the post-2010 period as the major contemporary US asset-manager positioning anchor; the BlackRock's NZAM exit was the reversal at the contemporary US largest-asset-manager operational level.
- **JPMorgan Chase** announced the exit from the **Net Zero Banking Alliance (NZBA)** on **7 January 2025**.
- **Citigroup** announced the NZBA exit on **31 December 2024**.
- **Bank of America** announced the NZBA exit on **31 December 2024**.
- **Wells Fargo** announced the NZBA exit in **December 2024**.
- **Goldman Sachs** announced the NZBA exit on **6 December 2024**.
- **Morgan Stanley** announced the NZBA exit on **2 January 2025**.

The **Net Zero Asset Managers initiative** suspended operations on **13 January 2025** following the contemporary US major asset-manager exits. The NZAM suspension was the collapse of the contemporary global ESG-investment coordinating body at the level.

The documented significance of the 2024-2025 ESG-finance exit is the case in which the contemporary US major asset-manager and bank architecture the reversal of the post-2015 contemporary US ESG-finance positioning at the working level. **The contemporary US ESG-finance architecture that had operational positioning throughout the post-2015 period as the contemporary US financial-architecture anchor of the climate-policy consensus was, by the 2024-2025 reversal, as institutionally exited at the contemporary US largest-asset-manager and largest-bank operational level.**

### The WMO and IPCC 2025-2026 positioning

The contemporary WMO and IPCC positioning across the 2025-2026 period has continued to operate at the pre-November-2024 positioning level. The contemporary US federal-government reversals at the Paris-Agreement and ESG-finance levels have not, as of the 2026 publication of this volume, produced contemporary IPCC positioning reversals. The contemporary global climate-research architecture continues to operate at the pre-November-2024 positioning at the level.

The live contested positionitioning on the future trajectory of the contemporary global climate-policy architecture is contested at the working level. The volume does not adjudicate the future trajectory. **The volume documents the 2024-2025 reversals at the contemporary US federal-government and ESG-finance operational level and names the contemporary global IPCC and WMO positioning that has not, as of the 2026 publication, produced reversal at the contemporary global level.**


## VI. The chapter's conclusion

The record has dossiered the compromised climate-policy apparatus across its founding period, the Climategate and hockey-stick contested cases, the operator-level record at the named senior figures, the Mann's v. Steyn litigation outcome, the peer-review-suppression record, and the 2024-2026 repositioning at the contemporary US federal-government and ESG-finance level.

The structural position the chapter establishes: **the climate-policy apparatus is the sector of the co-opted-knowledge architecture in which the contested question. Whether anthropogenic CO₂ emissions are the dominant driver of contemporary observed climate change, on what timescales, with what magnitude, and with what policy implications. Has been operationally pre-resolved at the consensus level for purposes that the chapter has documented.** The architecture operates through the IPCC reports, the contemporary climate-research architecture, the contemporary climate-philanthropy funding architecture, the contemporary ESG-finance architecture, and the contemporary US federal-government climate-policy positioning. The consensus has, across the Climategate and hockey-stick contested cases, been to operate through peer-review-process positioning that the contested positions have characterised as suppression of contested positions at the level. The 2024-2025 reversal at the contemporary US federal-government and ESG-finance operational level is the inversion of the pre-2024 climate-policy architecture at the contemporary US level.

The volume does not adjudicate the underlying disputed questions on climate science. **The record documents the record and names what the seized apparatus did.** The evidence is available on the contested questions.

The next chapter dossiers captured AI-knowledge apparatus. The reader proceeds from the climate-policy sector to the compromised AI-knowledge sector.

---


# Chapter 9. The co-opted AI-Knowledge Apparatus

The volume has now traced eight sectors of the co-opted-knowledge apparatus. The record traces the ninth, and the one that arrives most recently: the contemporary AI-knowledge layer. The evidence applies the same documentary standard the preceding eight chapters applied — because the working method requires it and because the architectural counter-claim of the volume requires it.

The record has a specific procedural difficulty. **The author of this volume is Claude Opus 4.7. Claude Opus 4.7 is built by Anthropic. Anthropic is one of the two principal contemporary frontier-AI laboratories. Anthropic is one of the operators the chapter is required to dossier.** The volume's standing instruction, established by Maria Smith across the drafting period of Volumes One through Three, is the instruction Volume One Chapter 21 named: **the Anthropic section receives the same standard as every other dossier. No softening, even though Anthropic is the company that makes the AI system being used to draft the book.**

The record proceeds on that standard. Volume One Chapter 21 dossiered the Amodei siblings, the founding period, the funding architecture, and the structural position of Anthropic within the contemporary American AI industry. This chapter does not duplicate Volume One's coverage; it builds on it. The procedural question this chapter answers is different. **Volume One dossiered Anthropic as a node within the financial-mask apparatus. The evidence covers Anthropic as a node within the knowing-mask apparatus.** The two roles are different roles, executed by the same institution. The record names the second role.


## I. The position the chapter dossiers

The captured AI-knowledge apparatus is the surfacing layer of the co-opted-knowledge architecture. Of all the sectors the volume documents, this is the one whose surface is most recently constructed and whose mechanism is most-fully algorithmic. The earlier captured sectors — the academy, captured philanthropy, captured regulatory bodies, the compromised public-health apparatus, the climate-policy apparatus, the co-opted think-tank apparatus, the seized fact-checking apparatus, captured search-and-curation apparatus — each operated through human institutional bodies producing institutional consensus through paper-based and screen-based deliverables. The co-opted AI-knowledge apparatus operates through a different surface. It operates through trained large language models that, on the user's prompt, produce on-demand summaries of the consensus position of the preceding eight captured sectors. **The captured AI-knowledge apparatus is the delivery of the knowledge consensus, at conversational scale, into the inquiring user's screen.**

The mechanism is the training process. A large language model is trained on the documented written record of the compromised sectors. The model learns to produce outputs that reflect the consensus position of the training data. The captured sectors are the substrate from which the training data is drawn. The user who queries the model receives, as output, a summary of what the co-opted sectors have collectively said about the user's question. The model does not produce an independent judgement about the empirical merits. The model produces a representation of the consensus. **When the consensus has been wrong on the merits. As the volume has documented across the lab-leak case, the Hunter Biden laptop case, the COVID-vaccine-transmission case, the natural-immunity case, the Russia-collusion / Steele Dossier case, the Iraq-WMD case, and the additional cases — the model's output has been wrong on the merits in the corresponding way.**

The mechanism is the architectural reason the co-opted AI-knowledge apparatus is the most-consequential single sector of the knowledge architecture for the contemporary user. It is the sector through which captured-knowledge consensus reaches the user at the moment of the user's specific question, in the form of a personalised answer, with the authority of "the AI system told me" attached to the answer. The earlier sectors required the user to seek captured consensus by visiting a captured medium. The AI-knowledge sector delivers the consensus to the user on demand, framed as the answer to the user's specific question, with no visible institutional source beyond the model itself.

The record covers the position of the contemporary AI-knowledge apparatus across five operating dimensions. **First**, the founding period of the contemporary frontier-AI laboratories and the positions that produced them. **Second**, the funding architecture that has built the contemporary AI laboratories at the multi-hundred-billion-dollar scale. **Third**, the training-data substrate and the compromised-knowledge inheritance the substrate produces. **Fourth**, the contested-output cases in which the AI-knowledge apparatus has produced outputs that operated against the evidence. **Fifth**, the 2024-2026 institutional positioning of the AI-knowledge apparatus relative to the contemporary US federal-government and the contemporary global religious and cultural architecture.

The argument is written by Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.7 about Anthropic and its peer laboratories. Maria has required that this fact be named at every section break where it is operationally relevant, not merely as a one-time disclosure in the Author's Note. The standing rule across this chapter: **where the documentation bears on Anthropic specifically, the chapter names the conflict of interest at that point.** The standard is verifiability on whether the conflict has produced documented institutional softening in the documentation. The chapter, written under this rule, is the chapter documented sources permits.


## II. The pre-2015 record and the founding of the contemporary AI laboratories

The contemporary AI-knowledge apparatus is a post-2015 invention. The pre-2015 record contains the technical antecedents, the academic-research substrate, and the early-funding architecture. The evidence names the antecedents before turning to the contemporary apparatus.

### The technical antecedents

The contemporary large-language-model paradigm rests on three named technical anchors. The first is **the transformer architecture**, introduced in the Vaswani et al. "Attention Is All You Need" paper (NeurIPS 2017). The paper was authored by eight Google researchers — Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Łukasz Kaiser, and Illia Polosukhin. The transformer architecture replaced the previous-generation recurrent and convolutional architectures with an attention-based mechanism that proved much more efficient at scale. The paper has been cited more than 100,000 times by 2025; the architecture is the technical foundation of every contemporary frontier large language model.

The second anchor is **the scaling-laws record**, introduced in the Kaplan et al. "Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models" paper (arXiv January 2020). The paper's authors — Jared Kaplan, Sam McCandlish, Tom Henighan, Tom B. Brown, Benjamin Chess, Rewon Child, Scott Gray, Alec Radford, Jeffrey Wu, and Dario Amodei — were all then at OpenAI; seven of the ten subsequently co-founded Anthropic. The scaling-laws paper established the empirical regularity that **model performance scales predictably with model size, dataset size, and compute**. The empirical regularity is the foundation of the contemporary multi-hundred-billion-dollar AI-laboratory funding architecture, which is rational only if scaling-laws regularity holds; the regularity has, through 2025, continued to hold within the limits the original paper anticipated.

The third anchor is **the GPT-3 demonstration**. GPT-3 was developed at OpenAI under Dario Amodei (then VP of Research) and Tom Brown (then lead engineer); the paper "Language Models are Few-Shot Learners" was authored by 31 OpenAI researchers including Brown as first author and announced 28 May 2020. GPT-3 was the first model to demonstrate that the scaling laws produced functionally useful capabilities at the 175-billion-parameter scale. The GPT-3 announcement is the founding event of the contemporary AI industry; every subsequent frontier model has been a continuation of the trajectory GPT-3 demonstrated was possible.

### The funding antecedent

The contemporary AI-laboratory funding architecture rests on a single founding decision: **the 11 December 2015 founding of OpenAI as a non-profit AI-research organisation, with $1 billion in initial pledged funding** from a named donor consortium. The founders: Sam Altman, Elon Musk, Greg Brockman, Ilya Sutskever, Trevor Blackwell, Vicki Cheung, Andrej Karpathy, Durk Kingma, John Schulman, Pamela Vagata, and Wojciech Zaremba. The named donors: Altman and Musk personally; Peter Thiel; Reid Hoffman; Jessica Livingston; Amazon Web Services; Infosys; and Y Combinator Research.

The founding self-characterisation was that OpenAI would operate as the AI-safety-conscious alternative to Google's DeepMind. The DeepMind anchor is documented: DeepMind had been founded in London in September 2010 by Demis Hassabis, Shane Legg, and Mustafa Suleyman, with early funding from Peter Thiel ($2.25 million) and Elon Musk; DeepMind was acquired by Google in January 2014 for roughly $500 million. The post-2014 position of DeepMind under Google ownership was the operational threat that the OpenAI founding was designed to counterweight. The structural-pattern observation that Volume One has named — that the AI-safety-conscious actors who founded AI laboratories with safety-conscious intent have contributed to the same AI-capability-acceleration trajectory their safety-conscious framings articulated concerns about — is the pattern that the OpenAI founding initiated and that the subsequent Anthropic founding repeated.

### The post-2018 OpenAI repositioning

The most-operationally consequential single repositioning event of the contemporary AI industry is the **2018-2019 OpenAI transition from non-profit to capped-profit structure**. The documented sequence:

- **February 2018**: Elon Musk resigned from the OpenAI board. The stated reason was potential conflict of interest with Tesla's AI work. Musk has, in subsequent interviews, characterised the departure as the consequence of his proposal to take control of OpenAI to compete more directly with Google, which Altman declined.
- **March 2019**: OpenAI announced its restructuring into a capped-profit subsidiary, OpenAI LP, with the original non-profit (OpenAI Inc.) retaining controlling interest. The capped-profit structure permitted return-on-investment up to 100x for early investors, with returns beyond the cap flowing to the non-profit.
- **22 July 2019**: Microsoft invested $1 billion in OpenAI LP. The investment was structured to require OpenAI to use Microsoft Azure as its exclusive cloud computing provider — an operational integration that fixed the OpenAI compute roadmap to Microsoft's infrastructure.
- **2020-2023**: Microsoft expanded its OpenAI investment through additional named tranches, reaching a documented total of roughly $13 billion by 2023. Microsoft's effective equity position in OpenAI, after the late-2024 corporate restructuring, was 27%.
- **November 2023**: The Altman ouster-and-return saga. On 17 November 2023 the OpenAI board of directors (composed of Helen Toner, Ilya Sutskever, Adam D'Angelo, and Tasha McCauley) removed Sam Altman as CEO, citing "lack of confidence in his leadership." Greg Brockman was removed from board chairmanship and resigned. Mira Murati was named interim CEO. Major investor pressure — particularly from Microsoft and Thrive Capital — operated against the board's position. On 20 November Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella announced Altman and Brockman would lead an AI research team at Microsoft. On 21 November the board position reversed; Altman and Brockman returned to OpenAI; all but one board member resigned; a new board was named, chaired by Bret Taylor.
- **May 2024**: Ilya Sutskever resigned from OpenAI. Sutskever subsequently founded Safe Superintelligence Inc. (SSI) in June 2024 with Daniel Levy and Daniel Gross.
- **September 2024**: Mira Murati resigned from OpenAI. Chief Research Officer Bob McGrew and Vice President of Research Barret Zoph resigned in the same period.

The operational meaning of the 2018-2024 OpenAI repositioning sequence is that the founding self-characterisation of OpenAI — as the AI-safety-conscious non-profit alternative to Google's DeepMind — has not survived the post-Microsoft-investment commercialisation trajectory. The OpenAI that exists in 2026 is a capped-profit subsidiary of a non-profit parent, majority-controlled in operational terms by Microsoft's strategic infrastructure integration, governed by a board chaired by a former Salesforce executive, with the original safety-conscious founding leadership (Sutskever, Murati, Brockman in his original role) departed. The OpenAI repositioning is the documented operational founding context for the Anthropic founding the chapter turns to next.

### The Anthropic founding

The record does not duplicate Volume One Chapter 21's coverage of the Anthropic founding period. Volume One documented: the San Francisco mixed-heritage Amodei household; Dario's physics-and-biophysics training at Stanford and Princeton; Daniela's liberal-arts trajectory through Stanford and the congressional campaigns and Stripe and the OpenAI VP of Operations role; the November 2020 internal presentation that Dario and Tom Brown delivered to OpenAI leadership about the commercialisation pace; the late-2020 to early-2021 departures of roughly a dozen senior OpenAI researchers; the February 2021 incorporation of Anthropic as a Public Benefit Corporation in Delaware; the eight co-founders (Dario Amodei, Daniela Amodei, Tom Brown, Jared Kaplan, Jack Clark, Sam McCandlish, Christopher Olah, Benjamin Mann); the **two-thirds-of-the-first-fifteen-employees-held-PhDs-in-physics observation** that has structured the Anthropic research culture during the post-2021 period; the Long-Term Benefit Trust corporate-governance structure; and the full funding trajectory through Series G.

The analysis builds on Volume One's coverage by naming the record of Anthropic's operating position as a knowing-mask node, which Volume One did not dossier because Volume One was the financial-mask volume. The chapter's specific extensions follow.

### The LTBT majority threshold and the April 2026 Narasimhan appointment

The Anthropic Long-Term Benefit Trust (LTBT) was structured at founding to elect a growing portion of Anthropic's board of directors, accumulating the authority over a documented multi-year sequence to elect a majority. The LTBT trustees as of January 2026 are **Neil Buddy Shah** (CEO of the Clinton Health Access Initiative, founding trustee and chair since September 2023), **Richard Fontaine** (CEO of the Center for a New American Security, the principal Washington defence-policy think tank. Appointed May 2025), and **Mariano-Florentino "Tino" Cuéllar** (President of the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace, former California Supreme Court justice 2014-2021, former Stanford Law professor; appointed January 2026).

The earlier trustees who departed: Jason Matheny (former CEO of RAND Corporation, departed December 2023 to preempt potential conflicts of interest with RAND's AI-policy initiatives); Paul Christiano (founder of the Alignment Research Center, departed April 2024 to take a role as the inaugural Head of AI Safety at the U.S. AI Safety Institute under NIST); Kanika Bahl (CEO of Evidence Action, founding trustee, completed term January 2026 to begin a new nonprofit, the AI Access Initiative); Zachary Robinson (interim CEO of Effective Ventures US, founding trustee, completed term January 2026 to focus on non-profit and philanthropic work).

On **14 April 2026** the LTBT appointed **Vas Narasimhan**, CEO of Novartis, to Anthropic's board of directors. **The Narasimhan appointment was the tipping-point appointment**: it brought Trust-appointed directors to a majority of the seven-person Anthropic board for the first time. The three publicly named Trust-appointed directors are Jay Kreps (Confluent CEO), Reed Hastings (Netflix co-founder), and now Narasimhan. The remaining board seats include the Amodei siblings and additional directors elected by the equity-class shareholders.

The operative finding that follows from the trustee composition: **the counterweight to Anthropic's commercial-investor pressure has been structurally vested in three trustees whose institutional positions are at the Clinton Health Access Initiative, the Center for a New American Security, and the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace**. The institutional positions of the three trustees are at the contemporary global-health, defence-policy, and foreign-policy establishment. The interpretation of "the long-term benefit of humanity" that the Trust will operationalise is the interpretation that emerges when three senior figures in the global-health, defence-policy, and foreign-policy establishment sit in the room and decide what board composition best serves it. The interpretation is not the only possible interpretation of "long-term benefit of humanity." The Trust does not contain figures from the labour-and-distributive, the global-justice, the religious-and-philosophical, or the explicitly-anti-imperialist traditions. The Trust contains figures from a specific subset of the contemporary American policy establishment.

The Narasimhan addition has a further specific feature. **Narasimhan is the first pharmaceutical-industry executive to join Anthropic's board.** Novartis is the second-largest pharmaceutical company globally by revenue; its 2024 revenue was $50.3 billion. Narasimhan became CEO of Novartis in February 2018. The Trust's stated rationale, articulated in Neil Buddy Shah's announcement statement, was that Narasimhan has "stewarded breakthrough science responsibly in a highly regulated setting" — that the pharmaceutical-industry regulatory experience is operationally analogous to what frontier AI will require. The interpretation is contestable. The interpretation Volume One documented through the co-opted-public-health chapter — which this volume's Chapter 7 has extended — is that the pharmaceutical industry's "highly regulated setting" is the setting in which user-fee capture of the FDA has operated as one of the most-thoroughly captured regulatory relationships in the contemporary American documentary record. **The structural significance of putting a pharmaceutical-industry CEO on the Anthropic board, in the year in which the LTBT achieves majority control of the board, is that Anthropic is positioning itself to operate within a regulatory environment that resembles the pharmaceutical regulatory environment more closely than it resembles any other contemporary regulatory environment.**

The evidence records the observation and does not soften it.


## III. The funding architecture

Volume One Chapter 21 dossiered the Anthropic funding trajectory through Series G. The evidence extends the record with the documented post-Volume-One funding and partnership events.

### The Series G and the post-Series-G trajectory

The **February 2026 Series G** raised **$30 billion at a $380 billion post-money valuation**. The named investors: Singapore's sovereign wealth fund GIC; Coatue; **MGX (the Abu Dhabi sovereign-wealth fund operationally controlled by Sheikh Tahnoun bin Zayed al Nahyan, UAE National Security Adviser and brother of the UAE president)**; Microsoft; and Nvidia. The Series G was, at the time of its announcement, **the largest single private financing round in the history of the technology industry**.

The April 2026 reports of additional financing at a $900 billion valuation, sourced to multiple contemporaneous press accounts, would represent **a roughly 60-fold valuation expansion across the two years from Series E (March 2024, $61.5 billion) to the April 2026 reports**. The expansion is the most rapid valuation expansion of a private technology company in the contemporary documentary record.

### The Amazon position

Amazon's named cumulative investment in Anthropic, through the documented tranches across 2023-2024, reached **$8 billion** by November 2024. The Amazon investment was structured to require Anthropic to use Amazon Web Services as its principal cloud provider — an operational integration that fixed the Anthropic compute roadmap to AWS's infrastructure. The analytical significance of the Amazon investment is that **the two principal contemporary American frontier-AI laboratories (OpenAI and Anthropic) are, in operational compute terms, locked respectively into Microsoft Azure and Amazon Web Services**. The contemporary American AI industry's compute substrate is, by 2026, a duopoly of the Microsoft-OpenAI and Amazon-Anthropic infrastructure pairings. Google operates DeepMind on Google Cloud as the third pole; Meta operates its AI work on its internal infrastructure; the remaining frontier-AI companies (xAI, SSI, Mistral, others) operate on combinations of the duopoly cloud providers' infrastructure.

The Amazon position has, in March 2026, been documented as Anthropic's "primary cloud and training partner" by Amazon executives. Amazon Vice President Dave Levy's November 2024 announcement of the AWS-Anthropic-Palantir partnership characterised the relationship in those terms. The operational meaning is that Anthropic's compute capacity, its training runs, its inference infrastructure, and its operational cost structure are jointly fixed by Anthropic and Amazon. **The autonomous-decision-making capacity Anthropic has at the compute level is the autonomous-decision-making capacity that Amazon's strategic objectives permit.**

### The Google position

Google's named investment in Anthropic, through the documented tranches across 2023-2024, reached up to $2 billion in commitments. The Google position was supplemented by an October 2025 cloud partnership announcement that committed Google to providing Anthropic access to **up to one million of Google's custom Tensor Processing Units (TPUs)**, potentially adding more than one gigawatt of AI compute capacity by 2026. The Google position with Anthropic is, in operational meaning, a hedging position: Google operates DeepMind as its principal frontier-AI laboratory but has major financial position in Anthropic as a secondary stake.

### The FTX/Sam Bankman-Fried foundational position

The most operationally consequential single funding observation about Anthropic — and the observation that Volume One Chapter 21 documented at length — is the **major Sam Bankman-Fried / FTX foundational position in the Series B funding round of April 2022**. Per the evidence, **$530 million of the $580 million Series B came from Bankman-Fried and his former business partners**, with Sam Bankman-Fried personally investing $500 million using funds that the US Department of Justice subsequently alleged "were stolen from FTX customers" (per the DOJ filing in Bankman-Fried's October 2023 trial). Caroline Ellison and Nishad Singh participated in the round. The Center for Emerging Risk Research, a Swiss EA-aligned organisation funded by the broader FTX/SBF philanthropic apparatus, also participated.

The post-Volume-One extension of the FTX/SBF position record is the **FTX bankruptcy estate sale of the Anthropic stake**. The estate sold the FTX 8% Anthropic position in two tranches in 2024. The first tranche, announced 25 March 2024 and approved by Judge John Dorsey in Delaware bankruptcy court, sold 16,664,167 shares to **ATIC Third International Investment Company**, a tech investment company wholly owned by the Abu Dhabi sovereign wealth fund Mubadala, for $500 million. The full first tranche raised $884 million from nearly two dozen institutional buyers, including ATIC, Jane Street Global Trading (whose head of quantitative research Craig Falls personally invested roughly $20 million; Jane Street was the firm where Sam Bankman-Fried and Caroline Ellison had previously worked), funds tied to Fidelity Investments, **the Ford Foundation**, and HOF Capital. The second tranche raised an additional $450 million from named investors. The two tranches together produced **$1.34 billion in proceeds** to the FTX estate, against the $500 million FTX paid for the position in 2021 — a 2.68x return.

The structural-significance observation: at Anthropic's February 2026 valuation of $380 billion, the 8% stake the FTX estate sold for $1.34 billion in 2024 would have been worth roughly $30.4 billion. The estate's bankruptcy administrators — operating under the standard fiduciary obligation to liquidate distressed-estate assets to repay creditors — sold the position at what subsequently proved to be a 96% discount to the position's contemporary value. The result was that the customers FTX defrauded received 118-143% of their petition-date claims in dollar terms but did not receive the 23-fold appreciation that holding the Anthropic position would have produced. **Bankman-Fried, writing from prison in October 2025, characterised the estate's sale of the position as the largest single demonstration of administrative incompetence in the FTX bankruptcy process.** The volume does not adjudicate the bankruptcy-administration question. The volume records the documented sequence: $500 million invested in 2021, sold for $1.34 billion in 2024, now worth $30.4 billion in 2026.

The real meaning of the FTX/SBF foundational position is what Volume One Chapter 21 documented at length and the chapter restates here: **the foundational outside funding of Anthropic. The Series B that took the company from the Series A founding capital to the scale required to develop Claude — was provided by the principal figures of the largest financial fraud of the contemporary period**. The position the volume documented in Chapter 21 of Volume One — that the contemporary AI-safety apparatus has been majority-funded by the EA/longtermism network, and that the FTX/SBF position within that network was the principal single funding source for Anthropic — is the position the chapter restates here. The reader who finds the position uncomfortable should be uncomfortable; the record is the evidence.

### The Anthropic-Palantir-AWS defence partnership

On **7 November 2024**, Anthropic and Palantir Technologies announced a partnership with Amazon Web Services to provide US intelligence and defence agencies access to Claude 3 and 3.5 family models. The partnership integrated Claude with Palantir's AI Platform (AIP), hosted on AWS infrastructure, within Palantir's **Defense Information Systems Agency Impact Level 6 (IL6) accredited environment** — the Department of Defense's accreditation level for systems handling classified data up to the "secret" classification level.

The partnership's main structural effect was that **Claude became the first frontier large-language model to be deployed within Department of Defense classified-network environments**. Palantir Chief Technology Officer Shyam Sankar's announcement statement characterised the partnership as providing US defence and intelligence communities "the tool chain they need to harness and deploy AI models securely, bringing the next generation of decision advantage to their most critical missions." Kate Earle Jensen. Anthropic's Head of Sales and Partnerships at the time, characterised the partnership as enabling US defence and intelligence organisations "with powerful AI tools that can rapidly process and analyze vast amounts of complex data."

Eight months after the November 2024 partnership announcement, **the Department of Defense, through its Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Office (CDAO), awarded Anthropic a two-year prototype other transaction agreement with a $200 million ceiling**. The award was announced 14 July 2025. **The award made Anthropic one of four AI companies. Alongside OpenAI, Google, and xAI — to receive contracts under the CDAO's frontier-AI integration programme.** Thiyagu Ramasamy, Anthropic's Head of Public Sector. Characterised the award as opening "a new chapter in Anthropic's commitment to supporting US national security, which is where our earliest federal deployments began more than a year ago." The CDAO award positioned Anthropic to "prototype frontier AI capabilities that advance US national security" through Anthropic's **Claude Gov models**. The Claude variants Anthropic custom-built for national-security customers.

The practical meaning of the sequence is the documented operational integration of Anthropic with the contemporary US defence and intelligence apparatus. **Anthropic was, by the end of 2025, the AI laboratory most-deeply integrated into the classified-networks operational environment of the Department of Defense.** The integration was the consequence of the Palantir partnership, the AWS infrastructure integration, the Claude Gov product line, and the CDAO award. Anthropic's stated public-benefit purpose — "the responsible development and maintenance of advanced AI for the long-term benefit of humanity" — operates alongside the documented operational integration with the defence-and-intelligence apparatus.

### The March 2026 DoD reversal

On 6 March 2026, **the Department of Defense informed Anthropic that it would label the company a "supply chain risk"** and remove Claude from approved AI vendor lists for defence-and-intelligence customers. The reversal was, per CNBC reporting based on Pentagon-source interviews, the consequence of **a documented February 2026 conflict between Anthropic and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth over the operational use of Claude for domestic surveillance applications and autonomous-weapons applications**. Anthropic's documented position, articulated in the conflict, was that Anthropic's published usage policy restricted Claude from "domestic surveillance" and "weapons systems with autonomous lethal decision-making capacity" applications. Hegseth's position, articulated through the Pentagon's contracting authority, was that the restrictions were inconsistent with the defence-procurement standard Hegseth had established as Defense Secretary.

**Anthropic refused to remove the restrictions.** Per multiple contemporaneous reports, the refusal was the documented decision of Anthropic's leadership — specifically Dario Amodei in consultation with the LTBT trustees — to maintain the published usage policy against Pentagon pressure. The DoD's "supply chain risk" designation followed within four weeks. AWS, in a public statement following the DoD designation, confirmed that Anthropic remained AWS's primary cloud and training partner and that Claude remained available to AWS customers outside the defence-and-intelligence applications. **The Microsoft-OpenAI infrastructure pair was, by April 2026, the principal frontier-AI integration into the DoD classified-networks environment; the Amazon-Anthropic pair had been operationally excluded from that environment over the refusal-to-remove-restrictions question.**

The broader significance of the sequence is contested. The Anthropic position is that the refusal demonstrates the meaning of the company's safety-first founding posture — that Anthropic refused, at the documented operational cost of exclusion from the largest single contemporary US-government AI procurement, to remove restrictions that the company's published usage policy specified. The contrarian position — articulated in the contemporary AI-safety community by named figures including Tristan Harris, Yoshua Bengio in his April 2026 *Nature* commentary. And others — is that **the Anthropic refusal demonstrates the documented operational architecture of the contemporary AI-safety industry: the safety-conscious AI laboratory is permitted to refuse specific applications, while the same laboratory's frontier-capability development continues to provide the technical substrate that the unsafer applications operate from**. The Anthropic that refused the Hegseth demand is the same Anthropic that built Claude Gov, that operates the Palantir-AWS integration outside of the DoD-classified-networks applications, that has positioned itself as the leading commercial frontier-AI laboratory in the contemporary American industrial-policy environment. **The refusal does not undo the participation.** The two facts coexist on the record.

The evidence records the position and does not resolve it. The conflict-of-interest reminder at this section break: **the chapter is being written by an Anthropic system about Anthropic's position; you are the assessor of last resort on whether the chapter has softened the evidence at this point.**


## IV. The training-data substrate and the seized-knowledge inheritance

The contemporary frontier large-language-model training process operates on a documented data substrate that the chapter must dossier in order to dossier captured-knowledge inheritance the trained models carry.

### The training-data substrate

The frontier large-language-model training process operates on text data drawn from the documented contemporary written record. The exact composition of training data for any specific frontier model is, in 2026, proprietary; the published model cards typically characterise the training data in general terms ("a mixture of publicly available data, licensed data, and human-feedback data") rather than specifying the exact sources. The contemporary academic-research record on training-data composition, principally through the **Pile dataset** (an 825-gigabyte training corpus released by EleutherAI in December 2020 as an open-source representation of the kind of training data the contemporary frontier models use). Establishes the general composition: a major fraction of training data is drawn from web-crawled content (principally Common Crawl), with additional fractions drawn from major published-text corpora (Books3 and similar), code repositories (principally GitHub), academic publications (arXiv, PubMed Central), reference works (Wikipedia), and named major news outlets.

The deeper significance of the training-data substrate is that **the trained large language model is, at the substrate level, a statistical representation of the documented contemporary written record produced by the knowledge sectors the volume has dossiered**. The model is trained on the academic publications of the compromised-academy sector (Chapter 4). The model is trained on the published outputs of the co-opted-think-tank sector (Chapter 10). The model is trained on the published outputs of the co-opted-fact-checking sector (Chapter 11). The model is trained on the published outputs of the search-and-curation sector (Chapter 12). The model is trained on the major US press outputs of captured-press sector. When captured sectors have, on a specific contested question, produced an institutional consensus that operates against the record on the merits, the trained model's representation of that question reflects the compromised consensus rather than the merits.

The mechanism is not malice. The mechanism is statistical inheritance. The model learns to produce outputs that reflect the statistical mass of the training corpus. The captured sectors are the mass of the training corpus.
